([/ˈmɪʃɪɡən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Michigan.ogg "File:En-us-Michigan.ogg") [*MISH-ig-ən*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a peninsular [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") in the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes_region "Great Lakes region") region of the [Upper Midwestern](/wiki/Upper_Midwest "Upper Midwest") [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). It shares water and land boundaries with [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") to the northwest, [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") to the west, [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") and [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") to the southwest, [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") to the southeast, and the Canadian [province](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") of [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") to the east, northeast and north. With a population of 10.14 million[[1]](#cite_note-CensusQuick-1) and an area of 96,716 sq mi (250,490 km2), Michigan is the [tenth-largest](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population") state by population, the [11th-largest](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area") by area, and the largest by total area east of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River").[[b]](#cite_note-6) The state capital is [Lansing](/wiki/Lansing,_Michigan "Lansing, Michigan"), while its most populous city is [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"). The [Metro Detroit](/wiki/Metro_Detroit "Metro Detroit") region in [Southeast Michigan](/wiki/Southeast_Michigan "Southeast Michigan") is among the nation's most populous and largest metropolitan economies. Other important [metropolitan areas](/wiki/List_of_Michigan_metropolitan_areas "List of Michigan metropolitan areas") include [Grand Rapids](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_metropolitan_area "Grand Rapids metropolitan area"), [Flint](/wiki/Flint,_Michigan "Flint, Michigan"), [Ann Arbor](/wiki/Ann_Arbor,_Michigan "Ann Arbor, Michigan"), [Kalamazoo](/wiki/Kalamazoo%E2%80%93Portage_metropolitan_area "Kalamazoo–Portage metropolitan area"), the [Tri-Cities](/wiki/Saginaw%E2%80%93Midland%E2%80%93Bay_City_combined_statistical_area "Saginaw–Midland–Bay City combined statistical area"), and [Muskegon](/wiki/Muskegon,_Michigan "Muskegon, Michigan").

**Michigan** ([/ˈmɪʃɪɡən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Michigan.ogg "File:En-us-Michigan.ogg") [*MISH-ig-ən*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a peninsular [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") in the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes_region "Great Lakes region") region of the [Upper Midwestern](/wiki/Upper_Midwest "Upper Midwest") [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). It shares water and land boundaries with [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") to the northwest, [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") to the west, [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") and [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") to the southwest, [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") to the southeast, and the Canadian [province](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") of [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") to the east, northeast and north. With a population of 10.14 million[[1]](#cite_note-CensusQuick-1) and an area of 96,716 sq mi (250,490 km2), Michigan is the [tenth-largest](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population") state by population, the [11th-largest](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area") by area, and the largest by total area east of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River").[[b]](#cite_note-6) The state capital is [Lansing](/wiki/Lansing,_Michigan "Lansing, Michigan"), while its most populous city is [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"). The [Metro Detroit](/wiki/Metro_Detroit "Metro Detroit") region in [Southeast Michigan](/wiki/Southeast_Michigan "Southeast Michigan") is among the nation's most populous and largest metropolitan economies. Other important [metropolitan areas](/wiki/List_of_Michigan_metropolitan_areas "List of Michigan metropolitan areas") include [Grand Rapids](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_metropolitan_area "Grand Rapids metropolitan area"), [Flint](/wiki/Flint,_Michigan "Flint, Michigan"), [Ann Arbor](/wiki/Ann_Arbor,_Michigan "Ann Arbor, Michigan"), [Kalamazoo](/wiki/Kalamazoo%E2%80%93Portage_metropolitan_area "Kalamazoo–Portage metropolitan area"), the [Tri-Cities](/wiki/Saginaw%E2%80%93Midland%E2%80%93Bay_City_combined_statistical_area "Saginaw–Midland–Bay City combined statistical area"), and [Muskegon](/wiki/Muskegon,_Michigan "Muskegon, Michigan").

Michigan consists of two peninsulas: the heavily forested [Upper Peninsula](/wiki/Upper_Peninsula "Upper Peninsula") (commonly called "the U.P."), which juts eastward from northern Wisconsin, and the more populated Lower Peninsula, stretching north from Ohio and Indiana. The peninsulas are separated by the [Straits of Mackinac](/wiki/Straits_of_Mackinac "Straits of Mackinac"), which connects Lake Michigan and Lake Huron, and are linked by the 5-mile-long [Mackinac Bridge](/wiki/Mackinac_Bridge "Mackinac Bridge") along [Interstate 75](/wiki/Interstate_75 "Interstate 75"). Bordering four of the five [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") and [Lake St. Clair](/wiki/Lake_St._Clair "Lake St. Clair"), Michigan has the longest [freshwater](/wiki/Freshwater "Freshwater") coastline of any U.S. political subdivision, measuring 3,288 miles.[[5]](#cite_note-NOAA-CRM-7) The state ranks second behind [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") in water coverage by square miles and first in percentage, with approximately 42%, and it also contains 64,980 [inland lakes and ponds](/wiki/List_of_lakes_of_Michigan "List of lakes of Michigan").[[6]](#cite_note-8)[[7]](#cite_note-9)

The Great Lakes region has largely been inhabited for thousands of years by Indigenous peoples such as the [Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe "Ojibwe"), [Odawa](/wiki/Odawa "Odawa"), [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi"), and [Wyandot](/wiki/Wyandot_people "Wyandot people").
Some people contend that the region's name is derived from the Ojibwe word ᒥᓯᑲᒥ (mishigami),[[c]](#cite_note-10) meaning "large water" or "large lake".[[3]](#cite_note-MiB-pdf-3)[[8]](#cite_note-11) Others say that it comes from the Mishiiken Tribe of Mackinac Island, also called Michinemackinawgo by Ottawa historian Andrew Blackbird,[[9]](#cite_note-12) whose surrounding lands were referred to as Mishiiken-imakinakom, later shortened to Michilimackinac.

In the 17th century, French explorers claimed the area for [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"). French settlers and [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis "Métis") established forts and settlements.
After France's defeat in the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War") in 1762, the area came under British control and later the U.S. following the [Treaty of Paris (1783)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)"), though control remained disputed with Indigenous tribes until treaties between 1795 and 1842. The area was part of the larger [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory"); the [Michigan Territory](/wiki/Michigan_Territory "Michigan Territory") was organized in 1805.

Michigan was [admitted](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_date_of_admission_to_the_Union "List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union") as the 26th state on January 26, 1837, entering as a [free state](/wiki/Slave_states_and_free_states "Slave states and free states") and quickly developing into an industrial and trade hub that attracted European immigrants, particularly from [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), [Macedonia](/wiki/Macedonia_(region) "Macedonia (region)"), and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands").[[10]](#cite_note-13)
In the 1930s, migration from [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia") and the [Middle East](/wiki/History_of_Middle_Eastern_people_in_Metro_Detroit "History of Middle Eastern people in Metro Detroit") and the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)") of [Black Southerners](/wiki/Black_Southerners "Black Southerners") further shaped the state, especially in Metro Detroit.[[11]](#cite_note-14)[[12]](#cite_note-15)

Michigan has a diversified economy with a gross state product of $738.308 billion as of Q3 2025, ranking [14th](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_GDP "List of U.S. states and territories by GDP") among the 50 states.[[13]](#cite_note-BureauEco-16) Although the state has developed a diverse economy, in the early 20th century it became widely known as the center of the [U.S. automotive industry](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_the_United_States "Automotive industry in the United States"), which developed as a major national economic force. It is home to the country's [three major automobile companies](/wiki/Big_Three_(automobile_manufacturers) "Big Three (automobile manufacturers)") (whose headquarters are all in Metro Detroit). Once exploited for logging and mining, today the sparsely populated Upper Peninsula is important for tourism because of its abundance of natural resources.[[14]](#cite_note-17)[[15]](#cite_note-18) The Lower Peninsula is a center of [manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing"), [forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry"), [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"), [services](/wiki/Service_(economics) "Service (economics)"), and [high-tech](/wiki/High-tech "High-tech") industry.

## History

Main article: [History of Michigan](/wiki/History_of_Michigan "History of Michigan")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Michigan history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Michigan_history "Timeline of Michigan history").

When the first European explorers arrived, the most populous tribes were the [Algonquian peoples](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples"), which include the [Anishinaabe](/wiki/Anishinaabe "Anishinaabe") groups of [Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe "Ojibwe"), [Odaawaa/Odawa (Ottawa)](/wiki/Ottawa_(tribe) "Ottawa (tribe)"), and the [Boodewaadamii/Bodéwadmi (Potawatomi)](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi"). The three nations coexisted peacefully as part of a loose confederation called the [Council of Three Fires](/wiki/Council_of_Three_Fires "Council of Three Fires"). The Ojibwe, whose numbers are estimated to have been at least 35,000, were the largest.[[16]](#cite_note-19)

The Ojibwe Indians (also known as Chippewa in the U.S.), an Anishinaabe tribe, were established in Michigan's Upper Peninsula and [northern](/wiki/Northern_Michigan "Northern Michigan") and [central Michigan](/wiki/Central_Michigan "Central Michigan"). Bands also inhabited [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") and southern [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba"), Canada; and northern [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), and northern and north-central [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"). Smaller groups of Algonquian Indians like the [Noquet](/wiki/Noquet "Noquet") in the Upper Peninsula were present for thousands of years but subsequently absorbed by neighboring tribes before and during European contact.[[17]](#cite_note-20) The Ottawa Indians lived primarily south of the [Straits of Mackinac](/wiki/Straits_of_Mackinac "Straits of Mackinac") in northern, [western](/wiki/Western_Michigan "Western Michigan"), and [southern Michigan](/wiki/Southern_Michigan "Southern Michigan"), but also in southern Ontario, northern Ohio, and eastern Wisconsin. The Potawatomi were in southern and western Michigan, in addition to northern and central Indiana, northern Illinois, southern Wisconsin, and southern Ontario. Other Algonquian tribes in Michigan, in the south and east, were the [Mascouten](/wiki/Mascouten "Mascouten"), the [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee "Menominee"), the [Miami](/wiki/Miami_(tribe) "Miami (tribe)"), the [Sac](/wiki/Sac_people "Sac people") (or Sauk), and the [Meskwaki (Fox)](/wiki/Meskwaki "Meskwaki"). The [Wyandot](/wiki/Wyandot_people "Wyandot people") were an Iroquoian-speaking people in this area; they were historically known as the Huron by the French, and were the historical adversaries of the [Iroquois Confederation](/wiki/Iroquois_Confederation "Iroquois Confederation").[[18]](#cite_note-21)

### 17th century

Main articles: [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") and [Canada (New France)](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Pere_Marquette.JPG/250px-Pere_Marquette.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pere_Marquette.JPG)

*Père Marquette and the Indians* (1869), by Wilhelm Lamprecht

French *[voyageurs](/wiki/Voyageurs "Voyageurs")* and *[coureurs des bois](/wiki/Coureur_des_bois "Coureur des bois")* explored and settled in Michigan in the 17th century. The first Europeans to reach what became Michigan were those of [Étienne Brûlé](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Br%C3%BBl%C3%A9 "Étienne Brûlé")'s expedition in 1622. The first permanent European settlement was founded in 1668 on the site where Père [Jacques Marquette](/wiki/Jacques_Marquette "Jacques Marquette") established [Sault Ste. Marie, Michigan](/wiki/Sault_Ste._Marie,_Michigan "Sault Ste. Marie, Michigan"), as a base for Catholic missions.[[19]](#cite_note-22)[[20]](#cite_note-23) Missionaries in 1671–75 founded outlying stations at [Saint Ignace](/wiki/Saint_Ignace,_Michigan "Saint Ignace, Michigan") and [Marquette](/wiki/Marquette,_Michigan "Marquette, Michigan"). Jesuit missionaries were well received by the area's Indian populations, with few difficulties or hostilities. In 1679, [Robert Cavelier, Sieur de la Salle](/wiki/Robert_Cavelier,_Sieur_de_la_Salle "Robert Cavelier, Sieur de la Salle") built [Fort Miami](/wiki/Fort_Miami_(Michigan) "Fort Miami (Michigan)") at present-day [St. Joseph](/wiki/St._Joseph,_Michigan "St. Joseph, Michigan"). In 1691, the French established a trading post and Fort St. Joseph along the St. Joseph River at the present-day city of [Niles](/wiki/Niles,_Michigan "Niles, Michigan").

### 18th century

Main articles: [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"), [Treaty of Paris (1763)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)"), [Province of Quebec (1763–1791)](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763%E2%80%931791) "Province of Quebec (1763–1791)"), [Indian Reserve (1763)](/wiki/Indian_Reserve_(1763) "Indian Reserve (1763)"), [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), [Treaty of Paris (1783)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)"), [Northwest Ordinance](/wiki/Northwest_Ordinance "Northwest Ordinance"), and [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory")

In 1701, French explorer and army officer [Antoine de la Mothe Cadillac](/wiki/Antoine_de_la_Mothe_Cadillac "Antoine de la Mothe Cadillac") founded [Fort Pontchartrain du Détroit](/wiki/Fort_Pontchartrain_du_D%C3%A9troit "Fort Pontchartrain du Détroit") or "Fort Pontchartrain on-the-Strait" on the strait, known as the [Detroit River](/wiki/Detroit_River "Detroit River"), between lakes [St. Clair](/wiki/Lake_St._Clair "Lake St. Clair") and [Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie").[[21]](#cite_note-24) Cadillac had convinced [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV")'s chief minister, [Louis Phélypeaux, Comte de Pontchartrain](/wiki/Louis_Ph%C3%A9lypeaux,_Marquis_of_Ph%C3%A9lypeaux "Louis Phélypeaux, Marquis of Phélypeaux"), that a permanent community there would strengthen French control over the upper Great Lakes and discourage [British](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") aspirations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The hundred soldiers and workers who accompanied Cadillac built a fort enclosing one [arpent](/wiki/Arpent "Arpent") (about 0.85 acres (3,400 m2),[[22]](#cite_note-tbaytel-25)[[23]](#cite_note-histdet-26) the equivalent of just under 200 feet (61 m) per side) and named it [Fort Pontchartrain](/wiki/Fort_Pontchartrain "Fort Pontchartrain"). Cadillac's wife, Marie Thérèse Guyon, soon moved to Detroit, becoming one of the first European women to settle in what was considered the wilderness of Michigan. The town quickly became a major [fur-trading](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") and shipping post. The *[Église de Saint-Anne](/wiki/Ste._Anne_de_Detroit_Catholic_Church "Ste. Anne de Detroit Catholic Church")* (Catholic Church of Saint Anne) was founded the same year.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] While the original building does not survive, the congregation remains active.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Cadillac later departed to serve as the French governor of Louisiana from 1710 to 1716.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] French attempts to consolidate the fur trade led to the [Fox Wars](/wiki/Fox_Wars "Fox Wars"), in which the Meskwaki (Fox) and their allies fought the French and their Native allies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Fort_Michilimackinac_2022.jpg/250px-Fort_Michilimackinac_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fort_Michilimackinac_2022.jpg)

[Fort Michilimackinac](/wiki/Fort_Michilimackinac "Fort Michilimackinac") at the [Straits of Mackinac](/wiki/Straits_of_Mackinac "Straits of Mackinac")

At the same time, the French strengthened [Fort Michilimackinac](/wiki/Fort_Michilimackinac "Fort Michilimackinac") at the Straits of Mackinac to better control their lucrative fur-trading empire. By the mid-18th century, the French also occupied forts at present-day Niles and Sault Ste. Marie, though most of the rest of the region remained unsettled by Europeans. France offered free land to attract families to Detroit, which grew to 800 people in 1765. It was the largest city between Montreal and New Orleans.[[24]](#cite_note-27) French settlers also established small farms south of the Detroit River opposite the fort, near a Jesuit mission and Huron village.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/British_colonies_1763-76_shepherd1923.PNG/250px-British_colonies_1763-76_shepherd1923.PNG)](/wiki/File:British_colonies_1763-76_shepherd1923.PNG)

Map of [British America](/wiki/British_America "British America") showing the original boundaries of the [Province of Quebec](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec "Province of Quebec") and its [Quebec Act of 1774](/wiki/Quebec_Act_of_1774 "Quebec Act of 1774") post-annexation boundaries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Treaty_of_Paris_by_Benjamin_West_1783.jpg/250px-Treaty_of_Paris_by_Benjamin_West_1783.jpg)](/wiki/File:Treaty_of_Paris_by_Benjamin_West_1783.jpg)

*[Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(painting) "Treaty of Paris (painting)")*, by [Benjamin West](/wiki/Benjamin_West "Benjamin West") (1783), an unfinished painting of the American diplomatic negotiators of the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)") which brought official conclusion to the [Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") and gave possession of Michigan and other territory to the new United States

From 1660 until the end of French rule, Michigan was part of the Royal Province of [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France").[[d]](#cite_note-28) In 1760, [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") fell to the British forces, ending the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War") (1754–1763), the North American front of the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") in Europe. Under the [1763 Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)"), Michigan and the rest of New France east of the Mississippi River were ceded by defeated France to Great Britain.[[25]](#cite_note-29) After the [Quebec Act](/wiki/Quebec_Act "Quebec Act") was passed in 1774, Michigan became part of the British [Province of Quebec](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763-1791) "Province of Quebec (1763-1791)"). By 1778, Detroit's population reached 2,144 and it was the third-largest city in Quebec province.[[26]](#cite_note-30)

During the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), Detroit was an important British supply center. Most of the inhabitants were French-Canadians or American Indians, many of whom had been allied with the French because of long trading ties. Because of imprecise cartography and unclear language defining the boundaries in the [1783 Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)"), the British retained control of Detroit and Michigan after the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"). When Quebec split into Lower and Upper Canada in 1791, Michigan was part of [Kent County](/wiki/Kent_County,_Ontario "Kent County, Ontario"), Upper Canada. It held its first democratic elections in August 1792 to send delegates to the new provincial parliament at Newark (now [Niagara-on-the-Lake](/wiki/Niagara-on-the-Lake,_Ontario "Niagara-on-the-Lake, Ontario")).[[27]](#cite_note-SFarmer-31)

Under terms negotiated in the 1794 [Jay Treaty](/wiki/Jay_Treaty "Jay Treaty"), Britain withdrew from Detroit and Michilimackinac in 1796. It retained control of territory east and south of the Detroit River, which are now included in Ontario, Canada. Questions remained over the boundary for many years, and the United States did not have uncontested control of the Upper Peninsula and [Drummond Island](/wiki/Drummond_Island "Drummond Island") until 1818 and 1847, respectively.

### 19th century

Main articles: [Indiana Territory](/wiki/Indiana_Territory "Indiana Territory"), [Organic act § List of organic acts](/wiki/Organic_act#List_of_organic_acts "Organic act"), [Michigan Territory](/wiki/Michigan_Territory "Michigan Territory"), [Admission to the Union](/wiki/Admission_to_the_Union "Admission to the Union"), [List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_date_of_admission_to_the_Union "List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union"), and [Michigan in the American Civil War](/wiki/Michigan_in_the_American_Civil_War "Michigan in the American Civil War")

During the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), the commander of the [Army of the Northwest](/wiki/Army_of_the_Northwest_(United_States) "Army of the Northwest (United States)"), Brigadier General [William Hull](/wiki/William_Hull "William Hull"), surrendered Detroit and the Michigan Territory to the British after a nearly bloodless [siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Detroit "Siege of Detroit") in August 1812. An American attempt to retake Detroit in January 1813 resulted in a severe American defeat at the [Battle of Frenchtown](/wiki/Battle_of_Frenchtown "Battle of Frenchtown"). This battle, still ranked as the bloodiest ever fought in the state, had the highest number of American casualties of any battle of the war.[[28]](#cite_note-32)

Michigan was retaken by the Americans in September 1813 after the British abandoned Detroit following the [Battle of Lake Erie](/wiki/Battle_of_Lake_Erie "Battle of Lake Erie"). Forces led by Major General [William Henry Harrison](/wiki/William_Henry_Harrison "William Henry Harrison") reoccupied Detroit and launched an invasion of [Upper Canada](/wiki/Upper_Canada "Upper Canada") which culminated in a British defeat at the [Battle of the Thames](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Thames "Battle of the Thames"). The more northerly areas of Michigan such as [Fort Mackinac](/wiki/Fort_Mackinac "Fort Mackinac") were held by the British until the 1815 [Treaty of Ghent](/wiki/Treaty_of_Ghent "Treaty of Ghent") restored the old boundaries. A number of forts, including [Fort Wayne](/wiki/Fort_Wayne_(Detroit) "Fort Wayne (Detroit)"), were built by the United States in Michigan during the 19th century out of fears of renewed fighting with Britain.

The population grew slowly until the opening in 1825 of the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") through the Mohawk Valley in New York, connecting the Great Lakes to the Hudson River and New York City.[[29]](#cite_note-33) The new route attracted a large influx of settlers to the Michigan territory. They worked as farmers, lumbermen, shipbuilders, and merchants and shipped out grain, lumber, and iron ore. By the 1830s, Michigan had 30,000 residents, more than enough to apply and qualify for statehood.[[30]](#cite_note-34)

A constitutional convention of assent was held to lead the territory to statehood.[[31]](#cite_note-Progress-35) In October 1835 the people approved the constitution of 1835, thereby forming a state government.[[32]](#cite_note-centennial-36) [Congressional](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") recognition was delayed pending resolution of a boundary dispute with [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") known as the [Toledo War](/wiki/Toledo_War "Toledo War"). Congress awarded the "Toledo Strip" to Ohio. Michigan received the western part of the Upper Peninsula as a concession and formally entered the Union as a [free state](/wiki/Slave_and_free_states "Slave and free states") on January 26, 1837. The Upper Peninsula proved to be a rich source of lumber, iron, and copper. Michigan led the nation in lumber production from the 1850s to the 1880s. [Railroads](/wiki/History_of_railroads_in_Michigan "History of railroads in Michigan") became a major engine of growth from the 1850s onward, with [Detroit the chief hub](/wiki/History_of_Detroit "History of Detroit").

A second wave of French-Canadian immigrants settled in Michigan during the late 19th to early 20th century, working in lumbering areas in counties on the Lake Huron side of the Lower Peninsula, such as the Saginaw Valley, Alpena, and Cheboygan counties, as well as throughout the Upper Peninsula, with large concentrations in Escanaba and the [Keweenaw Peninsula](/wiki/Keweenaw_Peninsula "Keweenaw Peninsula").[[33]](#cite_note-37)

The first statewide meeting of the [Republican Party](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_Republican_Party "History of the United States Republican Party") took place on July 6, 1854, in [Jackson, Michigan](/wiki/Jackson,_Michigan "Jackson, Michigan"), where the party adopted its platform.[[34]](#cite_note-38)[[35]](#cite_note-39) The state was predominantly Republican until the 1930s, reflecting the political continuity of migrants from across the Northern Tier of New England and New York.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Michigan made [a significant contribution](/wiki/Michigan_in_the_American_Civil_War "Michigan in the American Civil War") to the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") in the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") and sent more than forty regiments of volunteers to the federal armies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 20th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Michigan_Naval_Militia_standing_at_attention.jpg/250px-Michigan_Naval_Militia_standing_at_attention.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michigan_Naval_Militia_standing_at_attention.jpg)

Sailors of the [Michigan Naval Militia](/wiki/Michigan_Naval_Militia "Michigan Naval Militia") standing at attention during a parade in the 1920s, following [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I")

Michigan's economy underwent a transformation at the turn of the 20th century. Many individuals, including [Ransom E. Olds](/wiki/Ransom_E._Olds "Ransom E. Olds"), [John](/wiki/John_Francis_Dodge "John Francis Dodge") and [Horace Dodge](/wiki/Horace_Elgin_Dodge "Horace Elgin Dodge"), [Henry Leland](/wiki/Henry_Leland "Henry Leland"), [David Dunbar Buick](/wiki/David_Dunbar_Buick "David Dunbar Buick"), [Henry Joy](/wiki/Henry_Bourne_Joy "Henry Bourne Joy"), [Charles King](/wiki/Charles_Brady_King "Charles Brady King"), and [Henry Ford](/wiki/Henry_Ford "Henry Ford"), provided the concentration of engineering know-how and technological enthusiasm to develop the [automotive industry](/wiki/History_of_the_automobile "History of the automobile").[[36]](#cite_note-40) Ford's development of the moving [assembly line](/wiki/Assembly_line "Assembly line") in [Highland Park](/wiki/Highland_Park,_Michigan "Highland Park, Michigan") marked a new era in transportation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Like the [steamship](/wiki/Steamship "Steamship") and railroad, mass production of automobiles was a far-reaching development. More than the forms of public transportation, the affordable automobile transformed private life. Automobile production became the major industry of Detroit and Michigan, and permanently altered the socioeconomic life of the United States and much of the world.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

With the growth, the auto industry created jobs in Detroit that attracted immigrants from Europe and migrants from across the United States, including both blacks and whites from the rural [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] By 1920, Detroit was the fourth-largest city in the U.S..[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Residential housing was in short supply, and it took years for the market to catch up with the population boom.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] By the 1930s, so many immigrants had arrived that more than 30 languages were spoken in the public schools, and [ethnic](/wiki/Ethnic "Ethnic") communities celebrated in annual heritage festivals.[[37]](#cite_note-41) Over the years immigrants and migrants contributed greatly to Detroit's diverse urban culture, including popular music trends. The influential [Motown Sound](/wiki/Motown "Motown") of the 1960s was led by a variety of individual singers and groups.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Grand Rapids](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan"), the second-largest city in Michigan, also became an important center of manufacturing. Since 1838, the city has been noted for its furniture industry.[[38]](#cite_note-42)[[39]](#cite_note-43) In the 21st century, it is home to five of the world's leading office furniture companies. Grand Rapids is home to a number of major companies including [Steelcase](/wiki/Steelcase "Steelcase"), [Amway](/wiki/Amway "Amway"), and [Meijer](/wiki/Meijer "Meijer"). Grand Rapids is also an important center for [GE Aviation Systems](/wiki/GE_Aviation_Systems "GE Aviation Systems").

Michigan held its first [United States presidential primary](/wiki/United_States_presidential_primary "United States presidential primary") election in 1910.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] With its rapid growth in industry, it was an important center of industry-wide union organizing, such as the rise of the [United Auto Workers](/wiki/United_Auto_Workers "United Auto Workers").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1920 [WWJ (AM)](/wiki/WWJ_(AM) "WWJ (AM)") in Detroit became the first radio station in the United States to regularly broadcast commercial programs. Throughout that decade, some of the country's largest and most ornate [skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Detroit "List of tallest buildings in Detroit") were built in the city. Particularly noteworthy are the [Fisher Building](/wiki/Fisher_Building "Fisher Building"), [Cadillac Place](/wiki/Cadillac_Place "Cadillac Place"), and the [Guardian Building](/wiki/Guardian_Building "Guardian Building"), each of which has been designated as a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") (NHL).

In 1927 a school bombing took place in [Clinton County](/wiki/Clinton_County,_Michigan "Clinton County, Michigan"). The [Bath School disaster](/wiki/Bath_School_disaster "Bath School disaster") resulted in the deaths of 38 schoolchildren and constitutes the deadliest mass murder in a school in U.S. history.[[40]](#cite_note-44)

Michigan converted much of its manufacturing to satisfy defense needs during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"); it manufactured 10.9% of the United States military armaments produced during the war, ranking second (behind [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)")) among the 48 states.[[41]](#cite_note-45)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Detroit_Skyline_1942d.jpg/250px-Detroit_Skyline_1942d.jpg)](/wiki/File:Detroit_Skyline_1942d.jpg)

[Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit") in the mid-twentieth century. At the time, the city was the fourth-largest U.S. metropolis by population, and held about one-third of the state's population.

Detroit continued to expand through the 1950s, at one point doubling its population in a decade. After World War II, housing was developed in suburban areas outside city cores to meet demand for residences. The federal government subsidized the construction of [interstate highways](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System "Interstate Highway System"), which were intended to strengthen military access, but also allowed commuters and business traffic to travel the region more easily. Since 1960, modern advances in the auto industry have led to increased automation, high-tech industry, and increased suburban growth. Longstanding tensions in Detroit culminated in the [Twelfth Street riot](/wiki/1967_Detroit_riot "1967 Detroit riot") in July 1967.

During the late 1970s and the early 1980s, increasing [fuel costs](/wiki/Gasoline_and_diesel_usage_and_pricing "Gasoline and diesel usage and pricing") and other factors made significantly more global competition and recession among families. Michigan lost a significant amount of [population](/wiki/Population "Population") due to global competition and the dramatic unavailability of manufacturing jobs.[[42]](#cite_note-46) Meanwhile, Michigan had increased use of technology, specifically when the [IBM Personal Computer](/wiki/IBM_Personal_Computer "IBM Personal Computer") started selling in the state, in which became mostly used at work.

### 21st century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Detroit_and_Windsor_skylines_from_Belle_Isle_2025-09-07.jpg/250px-Detroit_and_Windsor_skylines_from_Belle_Isle_2025-09-07.jpg)](/wiki/File:Detroit_and_Windsor_skylines_from_Belle_Isle_2025-09-07.jpg)

The [Detroit–Windsor](/wiki/Detroit%E2%80%93Windsor "Detroit–Windsor") area is North America's largest cross-border conurbation, with a combined population of approximately six million people

Michigan became the leading auto-producing state in the U.S., with the industry primarily located throughout the [Midwestern United States](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"); Ontario, Canada; and the Southern United States.[[43]](#cite_note-NAM-47) With almost ten million residents in 2010, Michigan is a large and influential state, ranking tenth in population among the fifty states. Detroit is the centrally located metropolitan area of the [Great Lakes megalopolis](/wiki/Great_Lakes_megalopolis "Great Lakes megalopolis") and the second-largest metropolitan area in the U.S. (after [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago")) linking the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") system.

The [Metro Detroit](/wiki/Metro_Detroit "Metro Detroit") area in [Southeast Michigan](/wiki/Southeast_Michigan "Southeast Michigan") is the state's largest metropolitan area (roughly 50% of the population resides there) and the eleventh largest in the United States. The [Grand Rapids metropolitan area](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_metropolitan_area "Grand Rapids metropolitan area") in Western Michigan is the state's fastest-growing metro area, with more than 1.3 million residents as of 2006[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Michigan&action=edit).

## Geography

Further information: [Geography of Michigan](/wiki/Geography_of_Michigan "Geography of Michigan"), [Protected areas of Michigan](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_Michigan "Protected areas of Michigan"), and [List of Michigan state parks](/wiki/List_of_Michigan_state_parks "List of Michigan state parks")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Grlakes_lawrence_map.png/250px-Grlakes_lawrence_map.png)](/wiki/File:Grlakes_lawrence_map.png)

Map of the [Saint Lawrence River/Great Lakes Watershed](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_River "Saint Lawrence River") in North America. Its drainage area includes the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes"), the world's largest system of freshwater lakes. The basin covers nearly all of Michigan.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Huron_National_Wildlife_Refuge_%287455778394%29.jpg/250px-Huron_National_Wildlife_Refuge_%287455778394%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Huron_National_Wildlife_Refuge_(7455778394).jpg)

The [Huron National Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Huron_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Huron National Wildlife Refuge"), one of the fifteen [federal wildernesses](/wiki/Wilderness_area "Wilderness area") in Michigan

Michigan consists of two peninsulas separated by the [Straits of Mackinac](/wiki/Straits_of_Mackinac "Straits of Mackinac"). The [45th parallel north](/wiki/45th_parallel_north "45th parallel north") runs through the state, marked by highway signs and the Polar-Equator Trail—[[44]](#cite_note-48) along a line including [Mission Point Light](/wiki/Mission_Point_Light "Mission Point Light") near [Traverse City](/wiki/Traverse_City,_Michigan "Traverse City, Michigan"), the towns of [Gaylord](/wiki/Gaylord,_Michigan "Gaylord, Michigan") and [Alpena](/wiki/Alpena,_Michigan "Alpena, Michigan") in the Lower Peninsula and [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee,_Michigan "Menominee, Michigan") in the Upper Peninsula. With the exception of two tiny areas drained by the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") by way of the [Wisconsin River](/wiki/Wisconsin_River "Wisconsin River") in the Upper Peninsula and by way of the [Kankakee](/wiki/Kankakee_River "Kankakee River")-[Illinois River](/wiki/Illinois_River "Illinois River") in the Lower Peninsula, Michigan is drained by the Great Lakes-[St. Lawrence](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River") watershed and is the only state with the majority of its land thus drained. No point in the state is more than six miles (9.7 km) from a natural water source or more than 85 miles (137 km) from a Great Lakes shoreline.[[45]](#cite_note-49)

The Great Lakes that border Michigan from east to west are [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie"), [Lake Huron](/wiki/Lake_Huron "Lake Huron"), [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") and [Lake Superior](/wiki/Lake_Superior "Lake Superior"). The state is bounded on the south by the states of [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") and [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana"), sharing land and water boundaries with both. Michigan's western boundaries are almost entirely water boundaries, from south to north, with [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") and [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") in Lake Michigan; then a land boundary with Wisconsin and the Upper Peninsula, that is principally demarcated by the [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee_River "Menominee River") and [Montreal Rivers](/wiki/Montreal_River_(Wisconsin-Michigan) "Montreal River (Wisconsin-Michigan)"); then water boundaries again, in Lake Superior, with Wisconsin and Minnesota to the west, capped around by the Canadian province of Ontario to the north and east.

The heavily forested [Upper Peninsula](/wiki/Upper_Peninsula_of_Michigan "Upper Peninsula of Michigan") is relatively mountainous in the west. The [Porcupine Mountains](/wiki/Porcupine_Mountains "Porcupine Mountains"), which are part of one of the oldest mountain chains in the world,[[46]](#cite_note-50) rise to an altitude of almost 2,000 feet (610 m) above sea level and form the watershed between the streams flowing into Lake Superior and Lake Michigan. The surface on either side of this range is rugged. The state's highest point, in the [Huron Mountains](/wiki/Huron_Mountains "Huron Mountains") northwest of Marquette, is [Mount Arvon](/wiki/Mount_Arvon "Mount Arvon") at 1,979 feet (603 m). The peninsula is as large as Connecticut, Delaware, Massachusetts, and Rhode Island combined but has fewer than 330,000 inhabitants. The people are sometimes called "Yoopers" (from "U.P.'ers"), and their speech (the "[Yooper dialect](/wiki/Yooper_dialect "Yooper dialect")") has been heavily influenced by the numerous [Scandinavian](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") and Canadian immigrants who settled the area during the lumbering and [mining boom](/wiki/Copper_mining_in_Michigan "Copper mining in Michigan") of the late 19th century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Mackinac_Island-View_from_Fort_Mackinac.jpg/250px-Mackinac_Island-View_from_Fort_Mackinac.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mackinac_Island-View_from_Fort_Mackinac.jpg)

[Mackinac Island](/wiki/Mackinac_Island "Mackinac Island"), an island and resort area at the eastern end of the [Straits of Mackinac](/wiki/Straits_of_Mackinac "Straits of Mackinac"). More than 80% of the island is preserved as [Mackinac Island State Park](/wiki/Mackinac_Island_State_Park "Mackinac Island State Park").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Lake_Michigan_Sleeping_Bear_Dunes.jpg/250px-Lake_Michigan_Sleeping_Bear_Dunes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lake_Michigan_Sleeping_Bear_Dunes.jpg)

[Sleeping Bear Dunes](/wiki/Sleeping_Bear_Dunes "Sleeping Bear Dunes"), along the northwest coast of the Lower Peninsula of Michigan

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Upper_Tahquamenon_Falls_Fall_2007.jpeg/250px-Upper_Tahquamenon_Falls_Fall_2007.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Upper_Tahquamenon_Falls_Fall_2007.jpeg)

The [Tahquamenon Falls](/wiki/Tahquamenon_Falls "Tahquamenon Falls") in the [Upper Peninsula](/wiki/Upper_Peninsula_of_Michigan "Upper Peninsula of Michigan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Pointe_Mouillee.jpg/250px-Pointe_Mouillee.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pointe_Mouillee.jpg)

The [Pointe Mouillee State Game Area](/wiki/Pointe_Mouillee_State_Game_Area "Pointe Mouillee State Game Area"), one of the 221 [state game and wildlife areas](/wiki/List_of_Michigan_state_game_and_wildlife_areas "List of Michigan state game and wildlife areas") in Michigan. It encompasses 7,483 acres of hunting, recreational, and protected wildlife and wetland areas at the mouth of the [Huron River](/wiki/Huron_River_(Michigan) "Huron River (Michigan)") at [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie"), as well as smaller outlying areas within the [Detroit River](/wiki/Detroit_River "Detroit River").

The [Lower Peninsula](/wiki/Lower_Peninsula_of_Michigan "Lower Peninsula of Michigan") is shaped like a mitten and many residents hold up a hand to depict where they are from.[[47]](#cite_note-keilman20111209-51) It is 277 miles (446 km) long from north to south and 195 miles (314 km) from east to west and occupies nearly two-thirds of the state's land area. The surface of the peninsula is generally level, broken by conical hills and glacial [moraines](/wiki/Moraine "Moraine") usually not more than a few hundred feet tall. It is divided by a low water divide running north and south. The larger portion of the state is on the west of this and gradually slopes toward Lake Michigan. The highest point in the Lower Peninsula is either Briar Hill at 1,705 feet (520 m), or one of several points nearby in the vicinity of [Cadillac](/wiki/Cadillac,_Michigan "Cadillac, Michigan"). The lowest point is the surface of Lake Erie at 571 feet (174 m).

The geographic orientation of Michigan's peninsulas makes for a long distance between the ends of the state. [Ironwood](/wiki/Ironwood,_Michigan "Ironwood, Michigan"), in the far western Upper Peninsula, lies 630 miles (1,010 kilometers) by highway from [Lambertville](/wiki/Lambertville,_Michigan "Lambertville, Michigan") in the Lower Peninsula's southeastern corner. The geographic isolation of the Upper Peninsula from Michigan's political and population centers makes the region culturally and economically distinct. Frequent attempts to establish the Upper Peninsula as [its own state](/wiki/Superior_(proposed_U.S._state) "Superior (proposed U.S. state)") have failed to gain traction.[[48]](#cite_note-52)[[49]](#cite_note-53)

A feature of Michigan that gives it the distinct shape of a mitten is [the Thumb](/wiki/The_Thumb "The Thumb"), which projects into Lake Huron, forming [Saginaw Bay](/wiki/Saginaw_Bay "Saginaw Bay"). Other notable peninsulas of Michigan include the [Keweenaw Peninsula](/wiki/Keweenaw_Peninsula "Keweenaw Peninsula"), which projects northeasterly into Lake Superior from the Upper Peninsula and largely comprising Michigan's [Copper Country](/wiki/Copper_Country "Copper Country") region, and the [Leelanau Peninsula](/wiki/Leelanau_Peninsula "Leelanau Peninsula"), projecting from the Lower Peninsula into Lake Michigan, forming Michigan's "little finger".

Numerous lakes and [marshes](/wiki/Marsh "Marsh") mark both peninsulas, and the coast is much indented. Keweenaw Bay, [Whitefish Bay](/wiki/Whitefish_Bay "Whitefish Bay"), and the Big and [Little](/wiki/Little_Bay_de_Noc "Little Bay de Noc") Bays De Noc are the principal indentations on the Upper Peninsula. The Grand and [Little Traverse](/wiki/Little_Traverse_Bay "Little Traverse Bay"), [Thunder](/wiki/Thunder_Bay_(Michigan) "Thunder Bay (Michigan)"), and Saginaw bays indent the Lower Peninsula. Michigan has the second longest shoreline of any state—3,288 miles (5,292 km),[[50]](#cite_note-54) including 1,056 miles (1,699 km) of island shoreline.[[51]](#cite_note-55)

The state has [numerous large islands](/wiki/Islands_of_Michigan "Islands of Michigan"), the principal ones being the [North Manitou](/wiki/North_Manitou_Island "North Manitou Island") and [South Manitou](/wiki/South_Manitou_Island "South Manitou Island"), [Beaver](/wiki/Beaver_Island_(Lake_Michigan) "Beaver Island (Lake Michigan)"), and [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Islands_(Michigan) "Fox Islands (Michigan)") groups in Lake Michigan; [Isle Royale](/wiki/Isle_Royale "Isle Royale") and [Grande Isle](/wiki/Grand_Island_(Michigan) "Grand Island (Michigan)") in Lake Superior; Marquette, [Bois Blanc](/wiki/Bois_Blanc_Island_(Michigan) "Bois Blanc Island (Michigan)"), and [Mackinac](/wiki/Mackinac_Island "Mackinac Island") islands in Lake Huron; and [Neebish](/wiki/Neebish_Island "Neebish Island"), [Sugar](/wiki/Sugar_Island_(Michigan) "Sugar Island (Michigan)"), and Drummond islands in [St. Mary's River](/wiki/St._Marys_River_(Michigan-Ontario) "St. Marys River (Michigan-Ontario)"). Michigan has about 150 [lighthouses](/wiki/Lighthouse "Lighthouse"), the most of any U.S. state.[[52]](#cite_note-56) The first lighthouses in Michigan were built between 1818 and 1822. They were built to project light at night and to serve as a landmark during the day to safely guide the passenger ships and freighters traveling the Great Lakes (see: [lighthouses in the United States](/wiki/Lighthouses_in_the_United_States "Lighthouses in the United States")).

The [state's rivers](/wiki/List_of_Michigan_rivers "List of Michigan rivers") are generally small, short and shallow, and few are navigable. The principal ones include the Detroit River, St. Marys River, and [St. Clair River](/wiki/St._Clair_River "St. Clair River") which connect the Great Lakes; the [Au Sable](/wiki/Au_Sable_River_(Michigan) "Au Sable River (Michigan)"), [Cheboygan](/wiki/Cheboygan_River "Cheboygan River"), and [Saginaw](/wiki/Saginaw_River "Saginaw River"), which flow into Lake Huron; the [Ontonagon](/wiki/Ontonagon_River "Ontonagon River"), and [Tahquamenon](/wiki/Tahquamenon_River "Tahquamenon River"), which flow into Lake Superior; and the [St. Joseph](/wiki/St._Joseph_River_(Lake_Michigan) "St. Joseph River (Lake Michigan)"), [Kalamazoo](/wiki/Kalamazoo_River "Kalamazoo River"), [Grand](/wiki/Grand_River_(Michigan) "Grand River (Michigan)"), [Muskegon](/wiki/Muskegon_River "Muskegon River"), [Manistee](/wiki/Manistee_River "Manistee River"), and [Escanaba](/wiki/Escanaba_River "Escanaba River"), which flow into Lake Michigan. The state has 11,037 inland lakes—totaling 1,305 square miles (3,380 km2) of inland water—in addition to 38,575 square miles (99,910 km2) of Great Lakes waters. No point in Michigan is more than six miles (9.7 km) from an inland lake or more than 85 miles (137 km) from one of the Great Lakes.[[53]](#cite_note-57)

The state is home to several areas maintained by the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") including: [Isle Royale National Park](/wiki/Isle_Royale_National_Park "Isle Royale National Park"), in Lake Superior, about 30 miles (48 km) southeast of Thunder Bay, Ontario. Other national [protected areas](/wiki/Protected_areas "Protected areas") in the state include: [Keweenaw National Historical Park](/wiki/Keweenaw_National_Historical_Park "Keweenaw National Historical Park"), [Pictured Rocks National Lakeshore](/wiki/Pictured_Rocks_National_Lakeshore "Pictured Rocks National Lakeshore"), [Sleeping Bear Dunes National Lakeshore](/wiki/Sleeping_Bear_Dunes_National_Lakeshore "Sleeping Bear Dunes National Lakeshore"), [Huron National Forest](/wiki/Huron_National_Forest "Huron National Forest"), [Manistee National Forest](/wiki/Manistee_National_Forest "Manistee National Forest"), [Hiawatha National Forest](/wiki/Hiawatha_National_Forest "Hiawatha National Forest"), [Ottawa National Forest](/wiki/Ottawa_National_Forest "Ottawa National Forest") and [Father Marquette National Memorial](/wiki/Father_Marquette_National_Memorial "Father Marquette National Memorial"). The largest section of the [North Country National Scenic Trail](/wiki/North_Country_National_Scenic_Trail "North Country National Scenic Trail") passes through Michigan.

With 78 [state parks](/wiki/State_park "State park"), 19 state recreation areas, and six [state forests](/wiki/State_forests "State forests"), Michigan has the largest state park and [state forest](/wiki/State_forest "State forest") system of any state.

### Climate

See also: [Climate change in Michigan](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Michigan "Climate change in Michigan") and [List of Michigan tornadoes](/wiki/List_of_Michigan_tornadoes "List of Michigan tornadoes")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Michigan.png/250px-K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Michigan.png)](/wiki/File:K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Michigan.png)

[Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of Michigan, using 1991–2020 [climate normals](/wiki/Climatological_normal "Climatological normal")

Michigan has a [continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") with two distinct regions. The southern and central parts of the Lower Peninsula (south of Saginaw Bay and from the Grand Rapids area southward) have a warmer climate ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Dfa*) with hot summers and cold winters. The northern part of the Lower Peninsula and the entire Upper Peninsula has a more severe climate (Köppen *Dfb*), with warm, but shorter summers and longer, cold to very cold winters. Some parts of the state average high temperatures below freezing from December through February, and into early March in the far northern parts. During the winter through the middle of February, the state is frequently subjected to heavy [lake-effect snow](/wiki/Lake-effect_snow "Lake-effect snow"). The state averages from 30 to 40 inches (76 to 102 cm) of precipitation annually; however, some areas in the northern lower peninsula and the upper peninsula average almost 160 inches (4,100 mm) of snowfall per year.[[54]](#cite_note-58) Michigan's highest recorded temperature is 112 °F (44 °C) at [Mio](/wiki/Mio,_Michigan "Mio, Michigan") on July 13, 1936, and the coldest recorded temperature is −51 °F (−46 °C) at [Vanderbilt](/wiki/Vanderbilt,_Michigan "Vanderbilt, Michigan") on February 9, 1934.[[55]](#cite_note-netstate-59)

The state averages 30 days of thunderstorm activity per year. These can be severe, especially in the southern part of the state. The state averages 17 [tornadoes](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado") per year, which are more common in the state's extreme southern section. Portions of the southern border have been almost as vulnerable historically as states further west and in [Tornado Alley](/wiki/Tornado_Alley "Tornado Alley"). For this reason, many communities in the very southern portions of the state have tornado sirens to warn residents of approaching tornadoes. Farther north, in Central Michigan, Northern Michigan, and the Upper Peninsula, tornadoes are rare.[[56]](#cite_note-60)[[57]](#cite_note-Thunderstorm_hazards-61)

### Geology

The geological formation of the state is greatly varied, with the [Michigan Basin](/wiki/Michigan_Basin "Michigan Basin") being the most major formation. Primary boulders are found over the entire surface of the Upper Peninsula (being principally of primitive origin), while Secondary deposits cover the entire Lower Peninsula. The Upper Peninsula exhibits Lower [Silurian](/wiki/Silurian "Silurian") sandstones, limestones, copper and iron bearing rocks, corresponding to the Huronian system of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"). The central portion of the Lower Peninsula contains coal measures and rocks of the [Pennsylvanian](/wiki/Pennsylvanian_(geology) "Pennsylvanian (geology)") period. [Devonian](/wiki/Devonian "Devonian") and sub-Carboniferous deposits are scattered over the entire state.

Michigan rarely experiences [earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake"), and those that it does experience are generally smaller ones that do not cause significant damage. A 4.6-magnitude earthquake struck in August 1947. More recently, a 4.2-magnitude earthquake occurred on Saturday, May 2, 2015, shortly after noon, about five miles south of [Galesburg, Michigan](/wiki/Galesburg,_Michigan "Galesburg, Michigan") (9 miles southeast of [Kalamazoo](/wiki/Kalamazoo "Kalamazoo")) in central Michigan, about 140 miles west of Detroit, according to the Colorado-based [U.S. Geological Survey](/wiki/U.S._Geological_Survey "U.S. Geological Survey")'s [National Earthquake Information Center](/wiki/National_Earthquake_Information_Center "National Earthquake Information Center"). No major damage or injuries were reported, according to then-Governor [Rick Snyder](/wiki/Rick_Snyder "Rick Snyder")'s office.[[58]](#cite_note-62)

### Administrative divisions

Main article: [Administrative divisions of Michigan](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Michigan "Administrative divisions of Michigan")

See also: [List of counties in Michigan](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_Michigan "List of counties in Michigan"), [List of municipalities in Michigan](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Michigan "List of municipalities in Michigan"), and [List of Michigan metropolitan areas](/wiki/List_of_Michigan_metropolitan_areas "List of Michigan metropolitan areas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Michigan_Municipalities.png/250px-Michigan_Municipalities.png)](/wiki/File:Michigan_Municipalities.png)

[Michigan Municipalities](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Michigan "List of municipalities in Michigan")

State government is decentralized among three tiers—statewide, county and [township](/wiki/Civil_township "Civil township"). Counties are administrative divisions of the state, and townships are administrative divisions of a county. Both of them exercise state government authority, localized to meet the particular needs of their jurisdictions, as provided by state law. There are 83 [counties in Michigan](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_Michigan "List of counties in Michigan").[[59]](#cite_note-63)

Cities, [state universities](/wiki/State_university_system "State university system"), and villages are vested with [home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule") powers of varying degrees. Home rule cities can generally do anything not prohibited by law. The fifteen state universities have broad power and can do anything within the parameters of their status as educational institutions that is not prohibited by the state constitution. Villages, by contrast, have limited home rule and are not completely autonomous from the county and township in which they are located.

There are two types of township in Michigan: *general law* township and *charter*. [Charter township](/wiki/Charter_township "Charter township") status was created by the Legislature in 1947 and grants additional powers and stream-lined administration in order to provide greater protection against annexation by a city. As of April 2001[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Michigan&action=edit), there were 127 charter townships in Michigan. In general, charter townships have many of the same powers as a city but without the same level of obligations. For example, a charter township can have its own fire department, water and sewer department, police department, and so on—just like a city—but it is not *required* to have those things, whereas cities *must* provide those services. Charter townships can opt to use county-wide services instead, such as deputies from the county sheriff's office instead of a home-based force of ordinance officers.

Michigan 2020 population distribution

Since the [1800 U.S. census](/wiki/1800_United_States_census "1800 United States census"), Michigan has experienced relatively positive and stable population growth trends; beginning with a population of 3,757, the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") recorded 9,883,635 residents. At the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), its population was 10,077,331, an increase of 2.03% since 2010's tabulation. According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), it is the third-most populous state in the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") and its [East North Central](/wiki/East_North_Central_states "East North Central states") subregion, behind Ohio and Illinois.

The [center of population](/wiki/Center_of_population "Center of population") of Michigan is in [Shiawassee County](/wiki/Shiawassee_County,_Michigan "Shiawassee County, Michigan"), in the southeastern corner of the civil township of [Bennington](/wiki/Bennington_Township,_Michigan "Bennington Township, Michigan"), which is northwest of the village of [Morrice](/wiki/Morrice,_Michigan "Morrice, Michigan").[[63]](#cite_note-67)

According to the [American Immigration Council](/wiki/American_Immigration_Council "American Immigration Council") in 2019, an estimated 6.8% of Michiganders were immigrants, while 3.8% were native-born U.S. citizens with at least one immigrant parent.[[64]](#cite_note-:0-68) Numbering approximately 678,255 according to the 2019 survey, the majority of Michigander immigrants came from Mexico (11.5%), India (11.3%), Iraq (7.5%), China (5.3%), and Canada (5.3%); the primary occupations of its immigrants were technology, agriculture, and healthcare. Among its immigrant cohort, there were 108,105 undocumented immigrants, making up 15.9% of the total immigrant population.[[64]](#cite_note-:0-68)

According to [HUD](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Housing_and_Urban_Development "United States Department of Housing and Urban Development")'s 2022 [Annual Homeless Assessment Report](/wiki/Annual_Homeless_Assessment_Report_to_Congress "Annual Homeless Assessment Report to Congress"), there were an estimated 8,206 [homeless](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness") people in Michigan.[[65]](#cite_note-69)[[66]](#cite_note-70)

Ethnic origins in Michigan in 2021.

Since colonial European and American settlement, the majority of Michigan's population has been predominantly [non-Hispanic or non-Latino white](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_whites "Non-Hispanic whites"); [Americans of European descent](/wiki/Americans_of_European_descent "Americans of European descent") live throughout every county in the state, and most of Metro Detroit. Large European American groups include those of [German](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans"), [British](/wiki/British_Americans "British Americans"), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans"), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_Americans "Polish Americans") and [Belgian](/wiki/Belgian_Americans "Belgian Americans") ancestry.[[74]](#cite_note-78) [Scandinavian](/wiki/Nordic_and_Scandinavian_Americans "Nordic and Scandinavian Americans") and [Finnish Americans](/wiki/Finnish_Americans "Finnish Americans") have a notable presence in the Upper Peninsula.[[75]](#cite_note-79) [Western Michigan](/wiki/Western_Michigan "Western Michigan") is known for its [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Americans "Dutch Americans") heritage, especially in [Holland](/wiki/Holland,_Michigan "Holland, Michigan") and metropolitan Grand Rapids.[[76]](#cite_note-80)

[Black and African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans")—coming to Detroit and other northern cities in the Great Migration of the early 20th century—have formed a majority of the population in Detroit and other cities including [Flint](/wiki/Flint,_Michigan "Flint, Michigan") and [Benton Harbor](/wiki/Benton_Harbor,_Michigan "Benton Harbor, Michigan"). Since the 2021 census estimates—while Detroit was still the largest city in Michigan with a majority black population—it was no longer the largest black-majority city in the U.S., citing crime and higher-paying jobs given to whites.[[77]](#cite_note-81)[[78]](#cite_note-82)

As of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Michigan&action=edit), about 300,000 people in [Southeastern Michigan](/wiki/Southeastern_Michigan "Southeastern Michigan") trace their descent from the Middle East and Asia.[[79]](#cite_note-83) [Dearborn](/wiki/Dearborn,_Michigan "Dearborn, Michigan") has a sizeable [Arab American](/wiki/Arab_Americans "Arab Americans") community, with many [Assyrians](/wiki/Assyrian_Americans "Assyrian Americans"), and [Lebanese](/wiki/Lebanese_Americans "Lebanese Americans") who immigrated for jobs in the auto industry in the 1920s, along with more recent [Yemenis](/wiki/Yemeni_American "Yemeni American") and [Iraqis](/wiki/Iraqi_Americans "Iraqi Americans").[[80]](#cite_note-84) Dearborn also houses an [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_Americans "Armenian Americans") minority population, initially consisting of entirely young men.[[81]](#cite_note-85)[[82]](#cite_note-Woodfordp186187-86) There is a statue of [Komitas](/wiki/Komitas "Komitas"), an Armenian priest and composer, standing on Jefferson Avenue, in Detroit.[[83]](#cite_note-87) As of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Michigan&action=edit), almost 8,000 [Hmong people](/wiki/Hmong_people "Hmong people") lived in the state of Michigan, about double their 1999 presence in the state.[[84]](#cite_note-Kaiser2-88) Most lived in northeastern Detroit, but they had been increasingly moving to Pontiac and Warren.[[85]](#cite_note-Michdaily-89) By 2015, the number of Hmong in the Detroit city limits had significantly declined.[[86]](#cite_note-Rosenremaining-90) Lansing hosts a statewide Hmong New Year Festival.[[85]](#cite_note-Michdaily-89) The Hmong community also had a prominent portrayal in the 2008 film *[Gran Torino](/wiki/Gran_Torino "Gran Torino")*, which was set in Detroit.

As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Michigan&action=edit), 80% of Michigan's Japanese population lived in the counties of Macomb, Oakland, Washtenaw, and Wayne in the Detroit and Ann Arbor areas.[[87]](#cite_note-WilkinsonSookp158-91) As of April 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Michigan&action=edit), the largest Japanese national population is in [Novi](/wiki/Novi,_Michigan "Novi, Michigan"), with 2,666 Japanese residents, and the next largest populations are respectively in Ann Arbor, [West Bloomfield Township](/wiki/West_Bloomfield_Township,_Michigan "West Bloomfield Township, Michigan"), [Farmington Hills](/wiki/Farmington_Hills,_Michigan "Farmington Hills, Michigan"), and [Battle Creek](/wiki/Battle_Creek,_Michigan "Battle Creek, Michigan"). The state has 481 Japanese employment facilities providing 35,554 local jobs. 391 of them are in Southeast Michigan, providing 20,816 jobs, and the 90 in other regions in the state provide 14,738 jobs. The Japanese Direct Investment Survey of the [Consulate-General of Japan, Detroit](/wiki/Consulate-General_of_Japan,_Detroit "Consulate-General of Japan, Detroit") stated more than 2,208 additional Japanese residents were employed in the State of Michigan as of 1 October 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Michigan&action=edit), than in 2011.[[88]](#cite_note-92) During the 1990s, the Japanese population of Michigan experienced an increase, and many Japanese people with children moved to particular areas for their proximity to Japanese grocery stores and high-performing schools.[[87]](#cite_note-WilkinsonSookp158-91)

### Languages

In 2010, about 91.11% (8,507,947) of Michigan residents age five and older spoke only [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") at home, while 2.93% (273,981) spoke [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), 1.04% (97,559) [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), 0.44% (41,189) [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), 0.36% (33,648) [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") (which includes [Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese")), 0.31% (28,891) [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), 0.29% (27,019) [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), and [Syriac languages](/wiki/Assyrian_people#Language "Assyrian people") (such as [Modern Aramaic](/wiki/Neo-Aramaic_languages "Neo-Aramaic languages") and [Northeastern Neo-Aramaic](/wiki/Northeastern_Neo-Aramaic "Northeastern Neo-Aramaic")) was spoken as a [main language](/wiki/Main_language "Main language") by 0.25% (23,420) of the population over the age of five. In total, 8.89% (830,281) of Michigan's population age five and older spoke a [mother language](/wiki/Mother_language "Mother language") other than English.[[89]](#cite_note-MLA_Data-93) Since 2021, 90.1% of residents aged five and older spoke only English at home, and Spanish was the second-most spoken language with 2.9% of the population speaking it.[[90]](#cite_note-94)
