Following [British](/wiki/British_colonization_of_the_Americas "British colonization of the Americas") and [French colonization](/wiki/French_colonization_of_the_Americas "French colonization of the Americas") of the region surrounding Michigan, [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_United_States "Christianity in the United States") became the dominant religion, with [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism") historically being the largest single Christian group for the state. Until the 19th century, the Roman Catholic Church was the only organized religious group in Michigan, reflecting the territory's French colonial roots. Detroit's St. Anne's parish, established in 1701 by Antoine de la Mothe Cadillac, is the second-oldest Roman Catholic parish in the United States.[[92]](#cite_note-96) On March 8, 1833, the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") formally established a diocese in the Michigan territory, which included all of Michigan, Wisconsin, Minnesota, and the Dakotas east of the Mississippi River. When Michigan became a state in 1837, the boundary of the Roman Catholic Diocese of Detroit was redrawn to coincide with that of the state; the other dioceses were later carved out from the Detroit Diocese but remain part of the [Ecclesiastical Province](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Province "Ecclesiastical Province") of Detroit.[[93]](#cite_note-97) Several [Native American religions](/wiki/Native_American_religions "Native American religions") have been practiced in Michigan.

In 2020, there were 1,492,732 adherents of Roman Catholicism.[[94]](#cite_note-:1-98) There's also a significant [Independent Catholic](/wiki/Independent_Catholicism "Independent Catholicism") presence in Metro Detroit, including the [Ecumenical Catholic Church of Christ](/wiki/Ecumenical_Catholic_Church_of_Christ "Ecumenical Catholic Church of Christ") established by Archbishop Karl Rodig; the see of this church operates in a former Roman Catholic parish church.[[95]](#cite_note-99)[[96]](#cite_note-100)[[97]](#cite_note-101)

With the introduction of Protestantism to the state, it began to form the largest collective Christian group. In 2010, the Association of Religion Data Archives reported the largest Protestant denomination was the [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church") with 228,521 adherents;[[98]](#cite_note-www.thearda.com3-102) followed by the [Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod](/wiki/Lutheran_Church%E2%80%93Missouri_Synod "Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod") with 219,618, and the [Evangelical Lutheran Church in America](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_America "Evangelical Lutheran Church in America") with 120,598 adherents. The [Christian Reformed Church in North America](/wiki/Christian_Reformed_Church_in_North_America "Christian Reformed Church in North America") had almost 100,000 members and more than 230 congregations in Michigan.[[99]](#cite_note-103) The [Reformed Church in America](/wiki/Reformed_Church_in_America "Reformed Church in America") had 76,000 members and 154 congregations in the state.[[100]](#cite_note-104) By the 2020 study, [non- and inter-denominational](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity") Protestant churches formed the largest Protestant group in Michigan, numbering 508,904. The Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod grew to become the second-largest single Christian denomination, and United Methodists declined to being the third-largest. The Lutheran Protestant tradition was introduced by [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans") and Scandinavian immigrants. Altogether, Baptists numbered 321,581 between the [National Missionary Baptists](/wiki/National_Missionary_Baptist_Convention_of_America "National Missionary Baptist Convention of America"), [National Baptists](/wiki/National_Baptist_Convention,_USA,_Inc. "National Baptist Convention, USA, Inc."), [American Baptists](/wiki/American_Baptist_Churches_USA "American Baptist Churches USA"), [Southern Baptists](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention "Southern Baptist Convention"), [National Baptists of America](/wiki/National_Baptist_Convention_of_America_International,_Inc. "National Baptist Convention of America International, Inc."), [Progressive National Baptists](/wiki/Progressive_National_Baptist_Convention "Progressive National Baptist Convention"), and [Full Gospel Baptists](/wiki/Full_Gospel_Baptist_Church_Fellowship "Full Gospel Baptist Church Fellowship"); black Baptists formed the largest constituency.[[94]](#cite_note-:1-98) In West Michigan, Dutch immigrants fled from the specter of religious persecution and famine in the Netherlands around 1850 and settled in and around what is now Holland, Michigan, establishing a "colony" on American soil that fervently held onto Calvinist doctrine that established a significant presence of Reformed churches.[[101]](#cite_note-105)

In the same 2010 survey, Jewish adherents in the state of Michigan were estimated at 44,382, and Muslims at 120,351.[[102]](#cite_note-106) The first Jewish synagogue in the state was [Temple Beth El](/wiki/Temple_Beth_El_(Detroit,_Michigan) "Temple Beth El (Detroit, Michigan)"), founded by twelve German Jewish families in Detroit in 1850.[[103]](#cite_note-107) Islam was introduced by immigrants from the Near East during the 20th century.[[104]](#cite_note-108) Michigan is home to the largest mosque in North America, the [Islamic Center of America](/wiki/Islamic_Center_of_America "Islamic Center of America") in Dearborn. Battle Creek, Michigan, is also the birthplace of the [Seventh-day Adventist Church](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church"), which was founded on May 21, 1863.[[105]](#cite_note-Adventist_org-109)[[106]](#cite_note-New_indian-110)

## Economy

See also: [Michigan locations by per capita income](/wiki/Michigan_locations_by_per_capita_income "Michigan locations by per capita income") and [List of companies based in Michigan](/wiki/List_of_companies_based_in_Michigan "List of companies based in Michigan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Aerial_Tour_of_Gordie_Howe_Bridge_-_54603376784.jpg/250px-Aerial_Tour_of_Gordie_Howe_Bridge_-_54603376784.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_Tour_of_Gordie_Howe_Bridge_-_54603376784.jpg)

The [Detroit-Windsor](/wiki/Detroit-Windsor "Detroit-Windsor") border crossing is the busiest international border crossing in North America by trade volume, with approximately one-third of all [Canada–U.S. trade](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_trade_relations "Canada–United States trade relations") passing through this link. Pictured in the background is the under-construction [Gordie Howe International Bridge](/wiki/Gordie_Howe_International_Bridge "Gordie Howe International Bridge"), which will complement the existing [Ambassador Bridge](/wiki/Ambassador_Bridge "Ambassador Bridge"), shown in the foreground.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Detroit_Financial_District_from_Windsor%2C_Ontario%2C_2025-05-30.jpg/250px-Detroit_Financial_District_from_Windsor%2C_Ontario%2C_2025-05-30.jpg)](/wiki/File:Detroit_Financial_District_from_Windsor,_Ontario,_2025-05-30.jpg)

[Detroit Financial District](/wiki/Detroit_Financial_District "Detroit Financial District") in 2025

Michigan is among the wealthiest regions in the world. As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Michigan&action=edit), its [gross state product](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_GDP "List of U.S. states and territories by GDP") (GSP) stands at $738 billion ($72,898 per capita), ranking 14th among the 50 U.S. states.[[13]](#cite_note-BureauEco-16) Michigan's nominal GDP is larger than that of every nation in [Africa](/wiki/Economy_of_Africa "Economy of Africa") and all but three nations in [South America](/wiki/Economy_of_South_America "Economy of South America"). Its nominal per capita GDP exceeds all but two nations in the [European Union](/wiki/Economy_of_the_European_Union "Economy of the European Union"). When adjusted for [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity"), Michigan's per capita GDP is significantly higher than the European average, surpassing the figures of [France](/wiki/Economy_of_France "Economy of France"), [Germany](/wiki/Economy_of_Germany "Economy of Germany"), and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/Economy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Economy of the United Kingdom").

As of December 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Michigan&action=edit), the state recorded total [non-farm employment](/wiki/Nonfarm_payrolls "Nonfarm payrolls") of 4,543,300 across 315,965 employer establishments. Michigan is home to 15 [Fortune 500 companies](/wiki/Fortune_500_companies "Fortune 500 companies") as of 2024, and in 2025 alone, 152,800 new businesses were formed.[[107]](#cite_note-111) The state's 983,079 [small businesses](/wiki/Small_businesses "Small businesses") represent 99.6% of all businesses and employ 47.7% of the state's workforce.[[108]](#cite_note-112) As of May 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Michigan&action=edit), Michigan had an unemployment rate of 5.4%.[[109]](#cite_note-113)

The state's five largest employment sectors are education and health services; professional and business services; manufacturing; trade, transportation, and utilities; and leisure and hospitality. In output, the five largest sectors are manufacturing, followed by financial services; professional and business services; education and health services; and trade, transportation, and utilities.[[110]](#cite_note-114)

As the busiest international border crossing in North America by trade volume, the [Detroit–Windsor crossing](/wiki/Detroit%E2%80%93Windsor "Detroit–Windsor") plays a pivotal role in the [global supply chain](/wiki/Global_supply_chain_management "Global supply chain management"), handling approximately one third of all [Canada–U.S. trade](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_trade_relations "Canada–United States trade relations"). Additionally, the [Soo Locks](/wiki/Soo_Locks "Soo Locks"), which connect [Lake Superior](/wiki/Lake_Superior "Lake Superior") to the lower Great Lakes, is the world's busiest [lock system](/wiki/Lock_(water_navigation) "Lock (water navigation)") by tonnage, with 80 million tons passing through annually.[[111]](#cite_note-115)

Michigan's economy is heavily reliant on trade, with [international commerce](/wiki/International_business "International business") accounting for roughly 42% of the state's economy; Canada remains its largest trading partner.[[112]](#cite_note-116) Manufacturing, particularly the [automotive industry](/wiki/Automotive_industry "Automotive industry"), generates one seventh of the state's economy.[[113]](#cite_note-117) In 2023, Michigan exported $81 billion worth of goods.[[114]](#cite_note-:213-118) Transportation equipment, including motor vehicles and parts, is the state's top export, accounting for 45% of all exports in 2022.[[114]](#cite_note-:213-118) Michigan ranks first in the nation for the export of motor vehicles and parts.[[115]](#cite_note-119)

### Taxation and regulation

Michigan's personal [income tax](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax") is a flat rate of 4.25%. In addition, 24 cities impose income taxes; rates are set at 1% for residents and 0.5% for non-residents in all but four cities.[[116]](#cite_note-city-income-tax-120) Michigan's state [sales tax](/wiki/Sales_tax "Sales tax") is 6%, though items such as food and medication are exempted. [Property taxes](/wiki/Property_tax "Property tax") are assessed on the local level, but every property owner's local assessment contributes six [mills](/wiki/Mill_(currency) "Mill (currency)") (a rate of $6 per $1000 of property value) to the statutory State Education Tax. Property taxes are appealable to local boards of review and need the approval of the local electorate to exceed millage rates prescribed by state law and local charters. In 2011, the state repealed its business tax and replaced it with a 6% corporate income tax which substantially reduced taxes on business.[[117]](#cite_note-121)[[118]](#cite_note-122) Article IX of the Constitution of the State of Michigan also provides limitations on how much the state can tax.

A 6% [use tax](/wiki/Use_tax "Use tax") is levied on goods purchased outside the state (that are brought in and used in state), at parity with the sales tax.[[119]](#cite_note-123) The use tax applies to internet sales/purchases from outside Michigan and is equivalent to the sales tax.[[120]](#cite_note-124)

On December 20, 2019, Governor [Gretchen Whitmer](/wiki/Gretchen_Whitmer "Gretchen Whitmer") signed a package of bills into law effectively legalizing [online gambling](/wiki/Online_gambling "Online gambling") activities in Michigan, which allowed commercial and [tribal casinos](/wiki/Tribal_casinos "Tribal casinos") to apply for internet gaming licenses.[[121]](#cite_note-125)

### Agriculture

See also: [Michigan wine](/wiki/Michigan_wine "Michigan wine") and [Cherry production in Michigan](/wiki/Cherry_production_in_Michigan "Cherry production in Michigan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/ChateauChantal.JPG/250px-ChateauChantal.JPG)](/wiki/File:ChateauChantal.JPG)

[Chateau Chantal](/wiki/Chateau_Chantal "Chateau Chantal"), a winery and designated [American Viticultural Area](/wiki/American_Viticultural_Area "American Viticultural Area"), is located on the [Old Mission Peninsula](/wiki/Old_Mission_Peninsula "Old Mission Peninsula")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Michigan_Cherries%2C_2009_July.jpg/250px-Michigan_Cherries%2C_2009_July.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michigan_Cherries,_2009_July.jpg)

Michigan is the leading U.S. producer of [tart cherries](/wiki/Prunus_cerasus "Prunus cerasus"), [blueberries](/wiki/Blueberries "Blueberries"), [pickled cucumbers](/wiki/Pickled_cucumbers "Pickled cucumbers"), [navy beans](/wiki/Navy_beans "Navy beans"), and [petunias](/wiki/Petunias "Petunias")

A wide variety of commodity crops, fruits, and vegetables are grown in Michigan, making it second only to California among US states in the diversity of its agriculture.[[122]](#cite_note-126) The state has 54,800 farms utilizing 10,000,000 acres (40,000 km2) of land which sold $6.49 billion worth of products in 2010.[[123]](#cite_note-127) The most valuable agricultural product is milk. Leading crops include corn, soybeans, flowers, wheat, sugar beets, and potatoes. Livestock in the state included 78,000 sheep, a million cattle, a million hogs, and more than three million chickens. Livestock products accounted for 38% of the value of agricultural products while crops accounted for the majority.

Michigan is a leading grower of fruit in the US, including blueberries, [tart cherries](/wiki/Cherry_production_in_Michigan "Cherry production in Michigan"), apples, grapes, and peaches.[[124]](#cite_note-MIAG-128)[[125]](#cite_note-129)
Michigan produces 70 percent of the country's cherries. Most of these cherries are [Montmorency cherries](/wiki/Montmorency_cherries "Montmorency cherries").[[126]](#cite_note-130)
Plums, pears, and strawberries are also grown in Michigan. These fruits are mainly grown in [West Michigan](/wiki/West_Michigan "West Michigan") due to the moderating effect of Lake Michigan on the climate. There is also significant fruit production, especially cherries, but also grapes, apples, and other fruits, in northwest Michigan along Lake Michigan. Michigan produces [wines](/wiki/Michigan_wine "Michigan wine"), beers and a multitude of processed food products. [Kellogg's](/wiki/Kellogg%27s "Kellogg's") cereal is based in Battle Creek, Michigan and processes many locally grown foods. Thornapple Valley, [Ball Park Franks](/wiki/Ball_Park_Franks "Ball Park Franks"), [Koegel Meat Company](/wiki/Koegel_Meat_Company "Koegel Meat Company"), and [Hebrew National](/wiki/Hebrew_National "Hebrew National") sausage companies are all based in Michigan.

Michigan is home to very fertile land in the [Saginaw Valley](/wiki/Tri-Cities_(Michigan) "Tri-Cities (Michigan)") and Thumb areas. Products grown there include corn, sugar beets, navy beans, and soybeans. Sugar beet harvesting usually begins the first of October. It takes the sugar factories about five months to process the 3.7 million tons of sugarbeets into 485,000 tons of pure, white sugar.[[127]](#cite_note-131) Michigan's largest sugar refiner, [Michigan Sugar Company](/wiki/Michigan_Sugar_Company "Michigan Sugar Company")[[128]](#cite_note-132) is the largest east of the Mississippi River and the fourth largest in the nation. Michigan sugar brand names are Pioneer Sugar and the newly incorporated Big Chief Sugar. Potatoes are grown in Northern Michigan, and corn is dominant in Central Michigan. Alfalfa, cucumbers, and asparagus are also grown.

### Tourism

See also: [List of National Historic Landmarks in Michigan](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_Michigan "List of National Historic Landmarks in Michigan"), [List of Registered Historic Places in Michigan](/wiki/List_of_Registered_Historic_Places_in_Michigan "List of Registered Historic Places in Michigan"), and [List of museums in Michigan](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Michigan "List of museums in Michigan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Holland_MI_16M_%283858254690%29.jpg/250px-Holland_MI_16M_%283858254690%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Holland_MI_16M_(3858254690).jpg)

[Holland, Michigan](/wiki/Holland,_Michigan "Holland, Michigan"), is the home of the [Tulip Time Festival](/wiki/Tulip_Time_Festival "Tulip Time Festival"), the largest [tulip festival](/wiki/Tulip_festival "Tulip festival") in the U.S.

As of 2011, Michigan's tourists spent $17.2 billion per year in the state, supporting 193,000 tourism jobs.[[129]](#cite_note-133) Michigan's tourism website ranks among the busiest in the nation.[[130]](#cite_note-134) Destinations draw vacationers, hunters, and nature enthusiasts from across the United States and Canada. Michigan is over 50% forest land,[[131]](#cite_note-135) much of it quite remote. The forests, lakes and thousands of miles of beaches are top attractions. Event tourism draws large numbers to occasions like the [Tulip Time Festival](/wiki/Tulip_Time_Festival "Tulip Time Festival") and the [National Cherry Festival](/wiki/National_Cherry_Festival "National Cherry Festival").

In 2006, the Michigan State Board of Education mandated all public schools in the state hold their first day of school after Labor Day, in accordance with the new post-Labor Day school law. A survey found 70% of all tourism business comes directly from Michigan residents, and the Michigan Hotel, Motel, & Resort Association claimed the shorter summer between school years cut into the annual tourism season.[[132]](#cite_note-136) However, a bill introduced in 2023 would cancel this requirement, allowing individual districts to decide when their school year should begin.[[133]](#cite_note-137)[[134]](#cite_note-138)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/2007_Michigan_Mackinac_Island_Grand_Hotel_carriage.jpg/250px-2007_Michigan_Mackinac_Island_Grand_Hotel_carriage.jpg)](/wiki/File:2007_Michigan_Mackinac_Island_Grand_Hotel_carriage.jpg)

[Mackinac Island](/wiki/Mackinac_Island "Mackinac Island") is well known for cultural events and a wide variety of architectural styles, including the [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") [Grand Hotel](/wiki/Grand_Hotel_(Mackinac_Island) "Grand Hotel (Mackinac Island)").

[Tourism in metropolitan Detroit](/wiki/Tourism_in_metropolitan_Detroit "Tourism in metropolitan Detroit") draws visitors to leading attractions, especially [The Henry Ford](/wiki/The_Henry_Ford "The Henry Ford"), the [Detroit Institute of Arts](/wiki/Detroit_Institute_of_Arts "Detroit Institute of Arts"), the [Detroit Zoo](/wiki/Detroit_Zoo "Detroit Zoo"), and to [sports in Detroit](/wiki/Sports_in_Detroit "Sports in Detroit"). Other museums include the [Detroit Historical Museum](/wiki/Detroit_Historical_Museum "Detroit Historical Museum"), the [Charles H. Wright Museum of African American History](/wiki/Charles_H._Wright_Museum_of_African_American_History "Charles H. Wright Museum of African American History"), museums in the [Cranbrook Educational Community](/wiki/Cranbrook_Educational_Community "Cranbrook Educational Community"), and the [Arab American National Museum](/wiki/Arab_American_National_Museum "Arab American National Museum"). The metro area offers four major casinos, [MGM Grand Detroit](/wiki/MGM_Grand_Detroit "MGM Grand Detroit"), [Hollywood Casino](/wiki/Hollywood_Casino_at_Greektown "Hollywood Casino at Greektown"), [Motor City](/wiki/Motor_City_Casino "Motor City Casino"), and [Caesars Windsor](/wiki/Caesars_Windsor "Caesars Windsor") in Windsor, Ontario, Canada; moreover, Detroit is the largest American city and metropolitan region to offer casino resorts.[[135]](#cite_note-Mink-139)

Hunting and fishing are significant industries in the state. Charter boats are based in many Great Lakes cities to fish for salmon, trout, walleye, and perch. Michigan ranks first in the nation in licensed hunters (over one million) who contribute $2 billion annually to its economy. More than three-quarters of a million hunters participate in [white-tailed deer](/wiki/White-tailed_deer "White-tailed deer") season alone. Many school districts in rural areas of Michigan cancel school on the opening day of firearm deer season, because of attendance concerns.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[136]](#cite_note-140)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Downtown_Marquette%2C_Michigan_in_Autumn_%2843657514144%29.jpg/250px-Downtown_Marquette%2C_Michigan_in_Autumn_%2843657514144%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Downtown_Marquette,_Michigan_in_Autumn_(43657514144).jpg)

[Marquette, Michigan](/wiki/Marquette,_Michigan "Marquette, Michigan"), is home to a vast [snowmobile](/wiki/Snowmobile "Snowmobile") trail system.

Michigan's Department of Natural Resources manages the largest dedicated state forest system in the nation. The forest products industry and recreational users contribute $12 billion and 200,000 associated jobs annually to the state's economy. Public hiking and hunting access has also been secured in extensive commercial forests. The state has the highest number of golf courses and registered [snowmobiles](/wiki/Snowmobile "Snowmobile") in the nation.[[137]](#cite_note-141)

The state has numerous [historical markers](/wiki/Historical_marker "Historical marker"), which can themselves become the center of a tour.[[138]](#cite_note-142) The [Great Lakes Circle Tour](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Circle_Tour "Great Lakes Circle Tour") is a designated scenic road system connecting all of the Great Lakes and the St. Lawrence River.[[139]](#cite_note-143)

With its position in relation to the Great Lakes and the countless ships that have foundered over the many years they have been used as a transport route for people and bulk cargo, Michigan is a world-class scuba diving destination. The [Michigan Underwater Preserves](/wiki/Michigan_Underwater_Preserves "Michigan Underwater Preserves") are 13 underwater areas where wrecks are protected for the benefit of sport divers.

[Mackinac Island](/wiki/Mackinac_Island "Mackinac Island") Marina after the 2006 [Port Huron to Mackinac Boat Race](/wiki/Port_Huron_to_Mackinac_Boat_Race "Port Huron to Mackinac Boat Race")

Michigan's major-league sports teams include: [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers") baseball team, [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions") [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") team, [Detroit Red Wings](/wiki/Detroit_Red_Wings "Detroit Red Wings") [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") team, and the [Detroit Pistons](/wiki/Detroit_Pistons "Detroit Pistons") men's basketball team. The state also has a professional second-tier ([USL Championship](/wiki/USL_Championship "USL Championship")) soccer team in [Detroit City FC](/wiki/Detroit_City_FC "Detroit City FC"), which plays its home games at [Keyworth Stadium](/wiki/Keyworth_Stadium "Keyworth Stadium") in [Hamtramck, Michigan](/wiki/Hamtramck,_Michigan "Hamtramck, Michigan").

The Pistons played at Detroit's [Cobo Arena](/wiki/Cobo_Arena "Cobo Arena") until 1978 and at the [Pontiac Silverdome](/wiki/Pontiac_Silverdome "Pontiac Silverdome") until 1988 when they moved into [The Palace of Auburn Hills](/wiki/The_Palace_of_Auburn_Hills "The Palace of Auburn Hills"). The Detroit Lions played at [Tiger Stadium](/wiki/Tiger_Stadium_(Detroit) "Tiger Stadium (Detroit)") in Detroit until 1974, then moved to the Pontiac Silverdome where they played for 27 years between 1975 and 2002 before moving to [Ford Field](/wiki/Ford_Field "Ford Field") in Detroit in 2002. The Red Wings played at [Olympia Stadium](/wiki/Olympia_Stadium "Olympia Stadium") before moving to [Joe Louis Arena](/wiki/Joe_Louis_Arena "Joe Louis Arena") in 1979. Twenty-time Grand Slam champion [Serena Williams](/wiki/Serena_Williams "Serena Williams") was born in Saginaw. The [2011 World Champion for Women's Artistic Gymnastics](/wiki/2011_World_Artistic_Gymnastics_Championships "2011 World Artistic Gymnastics Championships"), [Jordyn Wieber](/wiki/Jordyn_Wieber "Jordyn Wieber") is from DeWitt. Wieber was also a member of the [gold medal team](/wiki/Gymnastics_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_artistic_team_all-around "Gymnastics at the 2012 Summer Olympics – Women's artistic team all-around") at the London Olympics in 2012.

Collegiate sports in Michigan are popular in addition to professional sports. The state's two largest athletic programs are the [Michigan Wolverines](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines "Michigan Wolverines") and [Michigan State Spartans](/wiki/Michigan_State_Spartans "Michigan State Spartans"). They compete in the [NCAA](/wiki/NCAA "NCAA") [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference") for most sports. The [Michigan High School Athletic Association](/wiki/Michigan_High_School_Athletic_Association "Michigan High School Athletic Association") features around 300,000 participants.

## Education

See also: [List of colleges and universities in Michigan](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_Michigan "List of colleges and universities in Michigan"), [List of high schools in Michigan](/wiki/List_of_high_schools_in_Michigan "List of high schools in Michigan"), and [List of school districts in Michigan](/wiki/List_of_school_districts_in_Michigan "List of school districts in Michigan")

Michigan's education system serves nearly 1.4 million K-12 students in public schools as of the 2024–25 school year.[[145]](#cite_note-149) In 2008–09, more than 124,000 students attend private schools and an uncounted number are [homeschooled](/wiki/Homeschooling_in_the_United_States "Homeschooling in the United States") under certain legal requirements.[[146]](#cite_note-150)[[147]](#cite_note-151) The public school system had a $14.5 billion budget in 2008–09.[[148]](#cite_note-152) From 2009 to 2019, over 200 [private schools](/wiki/Private_school "Private school") in Michigan closed, partly due to competition from [charter schools](/wiki/Charter_schools "Charter schools").[[149]](#cite_note-153) In 2022, *U.S. News & World Report* rated three Michigan high schools among the nation's 100 best: [City High Middle School](/wiki/City_High_Middle_School "City High Middle School") (18th), the [International Academy of Macomb](/wiki/International_Academy_of_Macomb "International Academy of Macomb") (21st), and the [International Academy](/wiki/International_Academy "International Academy") (52nd). [Washtenaw International High School](/wiki/Ypsilanti_Community_Schools "Ypsilanti Community Schools") ranked 107th.[[150]](#cite_note-154)

The [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan") is Michigan's oldest higher educational institution and among the oldest [research universities](/wiki/Research_universities "Research universities") in the nation. It was founded in 1817, 20 years before [Michigan Territory](/wiki/Michigan_Territory "Michigan Territory") achieved statehood.[[151]](#cite_note-155)[[152]](#cite_note-156)[[153]](#cite_note-157) [Kalamazoo College](/wiki/Kalamazoo_College "Kalamazoo College") is the state's oldest private liberal arts college, founded in 1833 by a group of [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") ministers as the Michigan and Huron Institute. From 1840 to 1850, the college operated as the Kalamazoo Branch of the University of Michigan. [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") settlers in [Spring Arbor Township](/wiki/Spring_Arbor_Township "Spring Arbor Township") founded [Albion College](/wiki/Albion_College "Albion College") in 1835. It is the state's second-oldest private liberal arts college.[[154]](#cite_note-158)

[Michigan Technological University](/wiki/Michigan_Technological_University "Michigan Technological University") is the first post-secondary institution in the [Upper Peninsula of Michigan](/wiki/Upper_Peninsula_of_Michigan "Upper Peninsula of Michigan"), founded in 1885 as the Michigan Mining School. [Eastern Michigan University](/wiki/Eastern_Michigan_University "Eastern Michigan University") was founded in 1849 as the Michigan State Normal School for the training of teachers.[[155]](#cite_note-159) It was the nation's fourth-oldest [normal school](/wiki/Normal_school#United_States "Normal school") and the first U.S. normal school outside [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"). In 1899, the Michigan State Normal School became the nation's first normal school to offer a four-year curriculum. [Michigan State University](/wiki/Michigan_State_University "Michigan State University") was founded in 1855 as the nation's first [agricultural college](/wiki/Agricultural_college "Agricultural college").

The [Carnegie Foundation](/wiki/Carnegie_Foundation_for_the_Advancement_of_Teaching "Carnegie Foundation for the Advancement of Teaching") classifies eight of the state's institutions (Michigan State University, Michigan Technological University, Eastern Michigan University, [Wayne State University](/wiki/Wayne_State_University "Wayne State University"), [Central Michigan University](/wiki/Central_Michigan_University "Central Michigan University"), [Western Michigan University](/wiki/Western_Michigan_University "Western Michigan University"), [Oakland University](/wiki/Oakland_University "Oakland University"), University of Michigan) as [research universities](/wiki/Research_universities "Research universities").[[156]](#cite_note-160)

The state of Michigan has six [MD-granting medical schools](/wiki/List_of_medical_schools_in_the_United_States "List of medical schools in the United States"): [Covenant HealthCare College of Medicine at Central Michigan University](/wiki/Central_Michigan_University "Central Michigan University"), [University of Michigan Medical School](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Medical_School "University of Michigan Medical School"), [Michigan State University College of Human Medicine](/wiki/Michigan_State_University_College_of_Human_Medicine "Michigan State University College of Human Medicine"), [Oakland University William Beaumont School of Medicine](/wiki/Oakland_University_William_Beaumont_School_of_Medicine "Oakland University William Beaumont School of Medicine"), [Wayne State University School of Medicine](/wiki/Wayne_State_University_School_of_Medicine "Wayne State University School of Medicine"), and [Western Michigan University Homer Stryker M.D. School of Medicine](/wiki/Western_Michigan_University_Homer_Stryker_M.D._School_of_Medicine "Western Michigan University Homer Stryker M.D. School of Medicine"). Additionally, Michigan is home to five [American Bar Association](/wiki/American_Bar_Association "American Bar Association") accredited [law schools](/wiki/List_of_law_schools_in_the_United_States "List of law schools in the United States"): [Michigan State University College of Law](/wiki/Michigan_State_University_College_of_Law "Michigan State University College of Law"), [Cooley Law School](/wiki/Cooley_Law_School "Cooley Law School"), [University of Detroit Mercy School of Law](/wiki/University_of_Detroit_Mercy_School_of_Law "University of Detroit Mercy School of Law"), [University of Michigan Law School](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Law_School "University of Michigan Law School"), and [Wayne State University Law School](/wiki/Wayne_State_University_Law_School "Wayne State University Law School").

In 2006, the [University Research Corridor](/wiki/University_Research_Corridor "University Research Corridor") in Michigan was formed with the University of Michigan, Michigan State University, and Wayne State University as members. It was renamed Research Universities for Michigan in May 2025, adding Michigan Technological University as its first new member.[[157]](#cite_note-University_Research_Corridor-161)[[158]](#cite_note-Bruns-162)[[159]](#cite_note-163)

## Infrastructure

### Energy

See also: [List of power stations in Michigan](/wiki/List_of_power_stations_in_Michigan "List of power stations in Michigan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Fermi_NPP.jpg/250px-Fermi_NPP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fermi_NPP.jpg)

[Enrico Fermi Nuclear Generating Station](/wiki/Enrico_Fermi_Nuclear_Generating_Station "Enrico Fermi Nuclear Generating Station") on the shore of [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie"), near [Monroe](/wiki/Monroe,_Michigan "Monroe, Michigan")

In 2020, Michigan consumed 113,740- [gigawatt-hours](/wiki/Kilowatt_hour#Watt-hour_multiples "Kilowatt hour") (GWh) of electrical energy and produced 116,700 (GWh) of electrical energy.[[160]](#cite_note-MI-ESRP-164)

[Coal power](/wiki/Coal_power "Coal power") is Michigan's leading source of electricity, producing roughly half its supply or 53,100 GWh of electrical energy (12.6 GW total capacity) in 2020.[[160]](#cite_note-MI-ESRP-164) Although Michigan has no active coal mines, coal is easily moved from other states by train and across the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") by [lake freighters](/wiki/Lake_freighter "Lake freighter"). The lower price of natural gas is leading to the closure of most coal plants, with Consumer Energy planning to close all of its remaining coal plants by 2025;[[161]](#cite_note-165) DTE plans to retire 2100MW of coal power by 2023.[[162]](#cite_note-166) The coal-fired [Monroe Power Plant](/wiki/Monroe_Power_Plant "Monroe Power Plant") in [Monroe](/wiki/Monroe,_Michigan "Monroe, Michigan"), on the western shore of [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie"), is the nation's 11th-largest electric plant, with a net capacity of 3,400 MW.

[Nuclear power](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power") is also a significant source of electrical power in Michigan, producing roughly one-quarter of the state's supply or 28,000-[gigawatt-hours](/wiki/Kilowatt_hour#Watt-hour_multiples "Kilowatt hour") (GWh) of electrical energy (4.3 GW total capacity) in 2020.[[160]](#cite_note-MI-ESRP-164) The three active nuclear power plants supply Michigan with about 26% of its electricity. [Donald C. Cook Nuclear Plant](/wiki/Donald_C._Cook_Nuclear_Plant "Donald C. Cook Nuclear Plant"), just north of [Bridgman](/wiki/Bridgman,_Michigan "Bridgman, Michigan"), is the state's largest [nuclear power plant](/wiki/Nuclear_power_plant "Nuclear power plant"), with a net capacity of 2,213 MW. The [Enrico Fermi Nuclear Generating Station](/wiki/Enrico_Fermi_Nuclear_Generating_Station "Enrico Fermi Nuclear Generating Station") is the second-largest, with a net capacity of 1,150 MW. It is also one of the two nuclear power plants in the [Detroit metropolitan area](/wiki/Detroit_metropolitan_area "Detroit metropolitan area") (within a 50-mile radius of Detroit's city center), about halfway between [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit") and [Toledo, Ohio](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio"), the other being the [Davis–Besse Nuclear Power Station](/wiki/Davis%E2%80%93Besse_Nuclear_Power_Station "Davis–Besse Nuclear Power Station"), in [Ottawa County, Ohio](/wiki/Ottawa_County,_Ohio "Ottawa County, Ohio"). The [Palisades Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Palisades_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Palisades Nuclear Power Plant"), south of [South Haven](/wiki/South_Haven,_Michigan "South Haven, Michigan"), closed in May 2022.[[163]](#cite_note-167) The [Big Rock Point Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Big_Rock_Point_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Big Rock Point Nuclear Power Plant"), Michigan's first nuclear power plant and the nation's fifth, was decommissioned in 1997.

Utility companies were required to generate at least 10% of their energy from renewable sources by 2015, under Public Act 295 of 2008. In 2016, the legislature set another mandate to reach at least 12.5% renewable energy by 2019 and 15% by end of year 2021, which all utilities subject to the law successfully met. By the end of 2022, Michigan had at least 6 GW of renewable generating capacity, and was projected to have at least 8 GW by the end of 2026. Wind energy accounted for 59% of all Michigan energy credits in 2021.[[164]](#cite_note-168)[[165]](#cite_note-169)

### Transportation

#### International crossings

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Lake_freighter_approaches_a_bridge_%2832304530671%29.jpg/250px-Lake_freighter_approaches_a_bridge_%2832304530671%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lake_freighter_approaches_a_bridge_(32304530671).jpg)

The [Blue Water Bridge](/wiki/Bluewater_Bridge "Bluewater Bridge"), a twin-span bridge across the [St. Clair River](/wiki/St._Clair_River "St. Clair River") that links [Port Huron](/wiki/Port_Huron "Port Huron") and [Sarnia, Ontario](/wiki/Sarnia,_Ontario "Sarnia, Ontario")

Michigan has seven international crossings with Ontario, Canada:

* [Ambassador Bridge](/wiki/Ambassador_Bridge "Ambassador Bridge"), North America's busiest international border, crossing the Detroit River
* [Blue Water Bridge](/wiki/Blue_Water_Bridge "Blue Water Bridge"), a twin-span bridge ([Port Huron, Michigan](/wiki/Port_Huron,_Michigan "Port Huron, Michigan"), and [Point Edward, Ontario](/wiki/Point_Edward,_Ontario "Point Edward, Ontario"), but the larger city of [Sarnia](/wiki/Sarnia "Sarnia") is usually referred to on the Canadian side)
* [Canadian Pacific Railway tunnel](/wiki/Michigan_Central_Railway_Tunnel "Michigan Central Railway Tunnel")
* [Detroit–Windsor Tunnel](/wiki/Detroit%E2%80%93Windsor_Tunnel "Detroit–Windsor Tunnel")
* [International Bridge](/wiki/Sault_Ste._Marie_International_Bridge "Sault Ste. Marie International Bridge") (Sault Ste. Marie, Michigan, and [Sault Ste. Marie, Ontario](/wiki/Sault_Ste._Marie,_Ontario "Sault Ste. Marie, Ontario"))
* [St. Clair River Railway Tunnel](/wiki/St._Clair_Tunnel "St. Clair Tunnel") (Port Huron and Sarnia)
* [Walpole–Algonac Ferry](/wiki/Walpole%E2%80%93Algonac_Ferry "Walpole–Algonac Ferry") ([Algonac, Michigan](/wiki/Algonac,_Michigan "Algonac, Michigan"), and [Walpole Island](/wiki/Walpole_Island "Walpole Island") First Nation, Ontario)

The [Gordie Howe International Bridge](/wiki/Gordie_Howe_International_Bridge "Gordie Howe International Bridge"), a second international bridge between Detroit and Windsor, is under construction. It is expected to be completed in early 2026.[[166]](#cite_note-170)[[167]](#cite_note-171)[[168]](#cite_note-172)[[169]](#cite_note-173)

Michigan only has one major ferry service still in operation with Ontario, the [Walpole–Algonac Ferry](/wiki/Walpole%E2%80%93Algonac_Ferry "Walpole–Algonac Ferry"), after the closures of the [Bluewater Ferry](/wiki/Sombra%E2%80%93Marine_City_(Bluewater)_Ferry "Sombra–Marine City (Bluewater) Ferry") and the [Detroit–Windsor Truck Ferry](/wiki/Detroit%E2%80%93Windsor_Truck_Ferry "Detroit–Windsor Truck Ferry").[[170]](#cite_note-174)

#### Railroads

See also: [List of Michigan railroads](/wiki/List_of_Michigan_railroads "List of Michigan railroads") and [History of railroads in Michigan](/wiki/History_of_railroads_in_Michigan "History of railroads in Michigan")

Michigan is served by four [Class I railroads](/wiki/Class_I_railroad "Class I railroad"): the [Canadian National Railway](/wiki/Canadian_National_Railway "Canadian National Railway"), the [Canadian Pacific Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway"), [CSX Transportation](/wiki/CSX_Transportation "CSX Transportation"), and the [Norfolk Southern Railway](/wiki/Norfolk_Southern_Railway "Norfolk Southern Railway"). These are augmented by several dozen [short line railroads](/wiki/Short_line_railroad "Short line railroad"). The vast majority of rail service in Michigan is devoted to [freight](/wiki/Freight_rail "Freight rail"), with Amtrak and various scenic railroads the exceptions.[[171]](#cite_note-175)

Main article: [Michigan Services](/wiki/Michigan_Services "Michigan Services")

Three [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") passenger rail routes serve the state. The [Pere Marquette](/wiki/Pere_Marquette_(Amtrak_train) "Pere Marquette (Amtrak train)") from Chicago to Grand Rapids, the [Blue Water](/wiki/Blue_Water_(train) "Blue Water (train)") from Chicago to Port Huron, and the [Wolverine](/wiki/Wolverine_(Amtrak_train) "Wolverine (Amtrak train)") from Chicago to Pontiac. There are plans for [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") for Detroit and its suburbs (see [SEMCOG Commuter Rail](/wiki/SEMCOG_Commuter_Rail "SEMCOG Commuter Rail")).[[172]](#cite_note-176)[[173]](#cite_note-177)[[174]](#cite_note-178)

See also: [Michigan State Trunkline Highway System](/wiki/Michigan_State_Trunkline_Highway_System "Michigan State Trunkline Highway System") and [County-Designated Highways in Michigan](/wiki/List_of_County-Designated_Highways_in_Michigan "List of County-Designated Highways in Michigan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/US2_Along_Lake_Michigan.jpg/250px-US2_Along_Lake_Michigan.jpg)](/wiki/File:US2_Along_Lake_Michigan.jpg)

[U.S. Highway 2](/wiki/U.S._Route_2_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 2 in Michigan") (US 2) runs along [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") from [Naubinway](/wiki/Naubinway_Island "Naubinway Island") to its eastern terminus at [St. Ignace](/wiki/St._Ignace,_Michigan "St. Ignace, Michigan").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Mackinac_Bridge_from_the_air3.jpg/250px-Mackinac_Bridge_from_the_air3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mackinac_Bridge_from_the_air3.jpg)

The [Mackinac Bridge](/wiki/Mackinac_Bridge "Mackinac Bridge"), a suspension bridge spanning the [Straits of Mackinac](/wiki/Straits_of_Mackinac "Straits of Mackinac") to connect the [Upper](/wiki/Upper_Michigan "Upper Michigan") and [Lower](/wiki/Lower_Michigan "Lower Michigan") peninsulas of Michigan

* [Interstate 75](/wiki/Interstate_75_in_Michigan "Interstate 75 in Michigan") (I-75) is the main thoroughfare between Detroit, Flint, and [Saginaw](/wiki/Saginaw,_Michigan "Saginaw, Michigan") extending north to [Sault Ste. Marie](/wiki/Sault_Ste._Marie,_Ontario "Sault Ste. Marie, Ontario") and providing access to Sault Ste. Marie, Ontario. The freeway crosses the [Mackinac Bridge](/wiki/Mackinac_Bridge "Mackinac Bridge") between the Lower and Upper Peninsulas. Auxiliary highways include [I-275](/wiki/Interstate_275_(Michigan) "Interstate 275 (Michigan)") and [I-375](/wiki/Interstate_375_(Michigan) "Interstate 375 (Michigan)") in Detroit; [I-475](/wiki/Interstate_475_(Michigan) "Interstate 475 (Michigan)") in Flint; and [I-675](/wiki/Interstate_675_(Michigan) "Interstate 675 (Michigan)") in Saginaw.
* [I-69](/wiki/Interstate_69_in_Michigan "Interstate 69 in Michigan") enters the state near the Michigan–Ohio–Indiana border, and it extends to [Port Huron](/wiki/Port_Huron,_Michigan "Port Huron, Michigan") and provides access to the [Blue Water Bridge](/wiki/Blue_Water_Bridge "Blue Water Bridge") crossing into [Sarnia](/wiki/Sarnia "Sarnia"), Ontario.
* [I-94](/wiki/Interstate_94_in_Michigan "Interstate 94 in Michigan") enters the western end of the state at the Indiana border, and it travels east to Detroit and then northeast to Port Huron and ties in with I-69. [I-194](/wiki/Interstate_194_(Michigan) "Interstate 194 (Michigan)") branches off from this freeway in Battle Creek. I-94 is the main artery between Chicago and Detroit.
* [I-96](/wiki/Interstate_96 "Interstate 96") runs east–west between Detroit and [Muskegon](/wiki/Muskegon,_Michigan "Muskegon, Michigan"). [I-496](/wiki/Interstate_496 "Interstate 496") loops through Lansing. [I-196](/wiki/Interstate_196 "Interstate 196") branches off from this freeway at Grand Rapids and connects to I-94 near Benton Harbor. [I-696](/wiki/Interstate_696 "Interstate 696") branches off from this freeway at [Novi](/wiki/Novi,_Michigan "Novi, Michigan") and connects to I-94 near [St. Clair Shores](/wiki/St._Clair_Shores,_Michigan "St. Clair Shores, Michigan").
* [U.S. Highway 2](/wiki/U.S._Route_2_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 2 in Michigan") (US 2) enters Michigan at the city of [Ironwood](/wiki/Ironwood,_Michigan "Ironwood, Michigan") and travels east to the town of [Crystal Falls](/wiki/Crystal_Falls,_Michigan "Crystal Falls, Michigan"), where it turns south and briefly re-enters Wisconsin northwest of [Florence](/wiki/Florence_(CDP),_Wisconsin "Florence (CDP), Wisconsin"). It re-enters Michigan north of [Iron Mountain](/wiki/Iron_Mountain,_Michigan "Iron Mountain, Michigan") and continues through the Upper Peninsula of Michigan to the cities of [Escanaba](/wiki/Escanaba,_Michigan "Escanaba, Michigan"), [Manistique](/wiki/Manistique,_Michigan "Manistique, Michigan"), and [St. Ignace](/wiki/St._Ignace,_Michigan "St. Ignace, Michigan"). Along the way, it cuts through the [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa_National_Forest "Ottawa National Forest") and [Hiawatha](/wiki/Hiawatha_National_Forest "Hiawatha National Forest") national forests and follows the northern shore of Lake Michigan. Its eastern terminus lies at exit 344 on I-75, just north of the Mackinac Bridge.
* [US 23](/wiki/U.S._Route_23_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 23 in Michigan") enters Michigan at the Ohio state line in the suburban spillover of [Toledo, Ohio](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio"), as a freeway and leads northward to Ann Arbor before merging with I-75 just south of Flint. Concurrent with I-75 through Flint, Saginaw, and Bay City, it splits from I-75 at [Standish](/wiki/Standish,_Michigan "Standish, Michigan") as an intermittently four-lane/two-lane surface road closely following the western shore of Lake Huron generally northward through Alpena before turning west to northwest toward Mackinaw City and I-75 again, where it terminates.
* [US 31](/wiki/U.S._Route_31_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 31 in Michigan") enters Michigan as Interstate-quality freeway at the Indiana state line just northwest of South Bend, Indiana, heads north to I-196 near Benton Harbor, and follows the eastern shore of Lake Michigan to Mackinaw City, where it has its northern terminus.
* [US 127](/wiki/U.S._Route_127_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 127 in Michigan") enters Michigan from Ohio south of [Hudson](/wiki/Hudson,_Michigan "Hudson, Michigan") as a two-lane, undivided highway and closely follows the [Michigan meridian](/wiki/Michigan_meridian "Michigan meridian"), the principal north–south line used to survey Michigan in the early 19th century. It passes north through [Jackson](/wiki/Jackson,_Michigan "Jackson, Michigan") and Lansing before terminating south of [Grayling](/wiki/Grayling,_Michigan "Grayling, Michigan") at I-75, and is a four-lane freeway for the majority of its course.
* [US 131](/wiki/U.S._Route_131 "U.S. Route 131") has its southern terminus at the Indiana Toll Road roughly one mile south of the Indiana state line as a two-lane surface road. It passes through Kalamazoo and Grand Rapids as a freeway of Interstate standard and continues as such to [Manton](/wiki/Manton,_Michigan "Manton, Michigan"), where it reverts to two-lane surface road to its northern terminus at US 31 in Petoskey.

### State government

Main article: [Government of Michigan](/wiki/Government_of_Michigan "Government of Michigan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Michigan_state_capitol.jpg/250px-Michigan_state_capitol.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michigan_state_capitol.jpg)

The [Michigan State Capitol](/wiki/Michigan_State_Capitol "Michigan State Capitol") in [Lansing](/wiki/Lansing "Lansing") houses the [legislative branch](/wiki/Michigan_Legislature "Michigan Legislature") of the government of the US state of Michigan.

Michigan is governed as a republic, with three [branches of government](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers"): the [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_branch "Executive branch") consisting of the [Governor of Michigan](/wiki/Governor_of_Michigan "Governor of Michigan") and the other independently elected constitutional officers; the [legislative branch](/wiki/Legislative_branch "Legislative branch") consisting of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/Michigan_State_House_of_Representatives "Michigan State House of Representatives") and [Senate](/wiki/Michigan_Senate "Michigan Senate"); and the [judicial branch](/wiki/Judicial_branch "Judicial branch"). The [Michigan Constitution](/wiki/Michigan_Constitution "Michigan Constitution") allows for the direct participation of the electorate by statutory [initiative](/wiki/Popular_initiative "Popular initiative") and [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum"), [recall](/wiki/Recall_election "Recall election"), and constitutional initiative and [referral](/wiki/Ratification "Ratification") (Article II, § 9,[[176]](#cite_note-180) defined as "the power to propose laws and to enact and reject laws, called the initiative, and the power to approve or reject laws enacted by the legislature, called the referendum. The power of initiative extends only to laws which the legislature may enact under this constitution"). [Lansing](/wiki/Lansing,_Michigan "Lansing, Michigan") is the [state capital](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States") and is home to all three branches of state government.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Michigan_House_of_Representatives.jpg/250px-Michigan_House_of_Representatives.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michigan_House_of_Representatives.jpg)

The floor of the [Michigan House of Representatives](/wiki/Michigan_House_of_Representatives "Michigan House of Representatives")

The governor and the other state constitutional officers serve four-year terms and may be re-elected only once. The current governor is [Gretchen Whitmer](/wiki/Gretchen_Whitmer "Gretchen Whitmer"). Michigan has two official [Governor's Residences](/wiki/Michigan_Governor%27s_Residence "Michigan Governor's Residence"); one is in Lansing, and the other is on [Mackinac Island](/wiki/Mackinac_Island "Mackinac Island"). The other constitutionally elected executive officers are the [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Michigan "Lieutenant Governor of Michigan"), who is elected on a joint ticket with the governor; the [secretary of state](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_of_Michigan "Secretary of State of Michigan"); and the [attorney general](/wiki/Attorney_General_of_Michigan "Attorney General of Michigan"). The lieutenant governor presides over the Senate (voting only in case of a tie) and is also a member of the cabinet. The secretary of state is the chief elections officer and is charged with running many licensure programs including motor vehicles, all of which are done through the branch offices of the secretary of state.

The [Michigan Legislature](/wiki/Michigan_Legislature "Michigan Legislature") consists of a 38-member Senate and 110-member House of Representatives. Members of both houses of the legislature are elected through [first past the post](/wiki/First_past_the_post "First past the post") elections by single-member electoral districts of near-equal population that often have boundaries which coincide with county and municipal lines. Senators serve four-year terms concurrent to those of the governor, while representatives serve two-year terms. The [Michigan State Capitol](/wiki/Michigan_State_Capitol "Michigan State Capitol") was dedicated in 1879 and has hosted the executive and legislative branches of the state ever since.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Michigan_National_Guard_members_participate_in_inauguration%2C_Adjutant_General_swearing-in_190101-F-VX133-049.jpg/250px-Michigan_National_Guard_members_participate_in_inauguration%2C_Adjutant_General_swearing-in_190101-F-VX133-049.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michigan_National_Guard_members_participate_in_inauguration,_Adjutant_General_swearing-in_190101-F-VX133-049.jpg)

Governor Gretchen Whitmer speaking at a National Guard ceremony in 2019

The Michigan judiciary consists of two courts with primary jurisdiction (the Circuit Courts and the District Courts), one intermediate level appellate court (the [Michigan Court of Appeals](/wiki/Michigan_Court_of_Appeals "Michigan Court of Appeals")), and the [Michigan Supreme Court](/wiki/Michigan_Supreme_Court "Michigan Supreme Court"). There are several administrative courts and specialized courts. District courts are trial courts of [limited jurisdiction](/wiki/Limited_jurisdiction "Limited jurisdiction"), handling most traffic violations, small claims, [misdemeanors](/wiki/Misdemeanor "Misdemeanor"), and civil suits where the amount contended is below $25,000. District courts are often responsible for handling the preliminary examination and for setting bail in felony cases. District court judges are elected to terms of six years. In a few locations, municipal courts have been retained to the exclusion of the establishment of district courts. There are 57 circuit courts in the State of Michigan, which have [original jurisdiction](/wiki/Original_jurisdiction "Original jurisdiction") over all civil suits where the amount contended in the case exceeds $25,000 and all criminal cases involving [felonies](/wiki/Felony "Felony"). Circuit courts are also the only trial courts in the State of Michigan which possess the power to issue [equitable remedies](/wiki/Equitable_remedies "Equitable remedies"). Circuit courts have [appellate jurisdiction](/wiki/Appellate_jurisdiction "Appellate jurisdiction") from district and municipal courts, as well as from decisions and decrees of state agencies. Most counties have their own circuit court, but sparsely populated counties often share them. Circuit court judges are elected to terms of six years. State appellate court judges are elected to terms of six years, but vacancies are filled by an appointment by the governor. There are four divisions of the Court of Appeals in Detroit, Grand Rapids, Lansing, and Marquette. Cases are heard by the Court of Appeals by panels of three judges, who examine the application of the law and not the facts of the case unless there has been grievous error pertaining to questions of fact. The Michigan Supreme Court consists of seven members who are elected on non-partisan ballots for staggered eight-year terms. The Supreme Court has original jurisdiction only in narrow circumstances but holds appellate jurisdiction over the entire state judicial system.

### Law

Main article: [Law of Michigan](/wiki/Law_of_Michigan "Law of Michigan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/712_michigan_hofj_edit.jpg/250px-712_michigan_hofj_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:712_michigan_hofj_edit.jpg)

[Michigan Supreme Court](/wiki/Michigan_Supreme_Court "Michigan Supreme Court") at the Hall of Justice

Michigan has had four constitutions, the first of which was ratified on October 5 and 6, 1835.[[177]](#cite_note-181) There were also constitutions from 1850 and 1908, in addition to the current constitution from 1963. The current document has a preamble, 11 articles, and one section consisting of a schedule and temporary provisions. The current constitution also includes a provision beginning in the general election held in 1978, and every 16 years thereafter, the question of a general revision of the constitution shall be submitted to the electors of the state, next scheduled to be considered November 2026.[[178]](#cite_note-182)[[179]](#cite_note-183) Michigan, like every U.S. state except [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana"), has a [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") legal system.

### Politics

Main article: [Politics of Michigan](/wiki/Politics_of_Michigan "Politics of Michigan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Michigan_Presidential_Election_Results_2024.svg/250px-Michigan_Presidential_Election_Results_2024.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Michigan_Presidential_Election_Results_2024.svg)

Democratic

Republican

Having been a [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)")-leaning state at the presidential level since the 1990s, Michigan has evolved into a [swing state](/wiki/Swing_state "Swing state") after [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") won the state in [2016](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election"). He then won it again in [2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"), after losing it by a slim 2.8% to Democrat [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") in [2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election"). Governors since the 1970s have alternated between the Democrats and [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), and statewide offices including [attorney general](/wiki/Michigan_Attorney_General "Michigan Attorney General"), [secretary of state](/wiki/Michigan_Secretary_of_State "Michigan Secretary of State"), and [senator](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") have been held by members of both parties in varying proportion. Additionally, from [1994](/wiki/1994_Michigan_gubernatorial_election "1994 Michigan gubernatorial election") until [2022](/wiki/2022_Michigan_gubernatorial_election "2022 Michigan gubernatorial election"), the governor-elect had always come from the party opposite the presidency. Following the [2024 elections](/wiki/2024_Michigan_elections "2024 Michigan elections"), control of [Michigan Legislature](/wiki/Michigan_Legislature "Michigan Legislature") is split, with the Democratic Party having a slim majority of two seats in the Senate while the Republican Party holds a 58-seat majority in the House. The state's [congressional delegation](/wiki/United_States_congressional_delegations_from_Michigan "United States congressional delegations from Michigan") is commonly split, with one party or the other typically holding a narrow majority; as of 2025 Republicans have a 7–6 majority.

Michigan was the home of [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford"), the 38th president of the United States. Born in Nebraska, he moved as an infant to Grand Rapids.[[180]](#cite_note-184)[[181]](#cite_note-ford-Nebraska-185) The [Gerald R. Ford Museum](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford_Museum "Gerald R. Ford Museum") is in Grand Rapids, and the [Gerald R. Ford Presidential Library](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford_Presidential_Library "Gerald R. Ford Presidential Library") is on the campus of his alma mater, the University of Michigan in Ann Arbor.

In a 2020 study, Michigan was ranked as the 13th easiest state for citizens to vote in.[[182]](#cite_note-186) Amendments to the constitution in 2020 and 2022 also provide for voting by mail, audits of statewide election results, and to vote free of harassment, threats, and intimidation.[[183]](#cite_note-187) The [Cato Institute](/wiki/Cato_Institute "Cato Institute") ranks Michigan 7th in its overall ranking for personal and economic freedom in the United States in the 2021 and 2023 editions of its *Freedom in the 50 States* index.[[184]](#cite_note-188) In 2022, Michigan voters passed an amendment recognizing abortion and contraceptive rights within the [state's constitution](/wiki/Michigan_Constitution "Michigan Constitution").[[185]](#cite_note-freep_passage-189)

## State symbols and nicknames

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Dwarf_Lake_Iris.jpg/250px-Dwarf_Lake_Iris.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dwarf_Lake_Iris.jpg)

[Dwarf lake iris](/wiki/Dwarf_lake_iris "Dwarf lake iris")

Michigan is traditionally known as "The Wolverine State", and the University of Michigan uses the wolverine as its mascot. The association is well and long established: for example, many Detroiters volunteered to fight during the American Civil War and [George Armstrong Custer](/wiki/George_Armstrong_Custer "George Armstrong Custer"), who led the Michigan Brigade, called them the "Wolverines". The origins of this association are obscure; it may derive from a busy trade in wolverine furs in Sault Ste. Marie in the 18th century or may recall a disparagement intended to compare early settlers in Michigan with the vicious mammal. Wolverines are, however, extremely rare in Michigan. A sighting in February 2004 near [Ubly](/wiki/Ubly,_Michigan "Ubly, Michigan") was the first confirmed sighting in Michigan in 200 years.[[186]](#cite_note-msnbc-190) Another wolverine was found dead in 2010.[[187]](#cite_note-191)

* [State nicknames](/wiki/State_nickname "State nickname"): *Wolverine State*, *Great Lakes State*, *Mitten State*, *Water-Winter Wonderland*
* [State motto](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_mottos "List of U.S. state mottos"): *[Si quaeris peninsulam amoenam circumspice](/wiki/Si_quaeris_peninsulam_amoenam_circumspice "Si quaeris peninsulam amoenam circumspice")* (Latin: "If you seek a pleasant peninsula, look about you") adopted in 1835 on the coat-of-arms, but never as an official motto. This is a paraphrase of the epitaph of British architect Sir [Christopher Wren](/wiki/Christopher_Wren "Christopher Wren") about his masterpiece, [St. Paul's Cathedral](/wiki/St._Paul%27s_Cathedral "St. Paul's Cathedral").[[188]](#cite_note-192)[[189]](#cite_note-193)
* [State song](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_songs "List of U.S. state songs"): "[My Michigan](/wiki/My_Michigan "My Michigan")" (official since 1937, but disputed amongst residents),[[190]](#cite_note-194) "[Michigan, My Michigan](/wiki/Michigan,_My_Michigan "Michigan, My Michigan")" (unofficial state song, since the civil war)
* [State bird](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_birds "List of U.S. state birds"): [American robin](/wiki/American_robin "American robin") (since 1931)
* [State animal](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_mammals "List of U.S. state mammals"): [wolverine](/wiki/Wolverine "Wolverine") (traditional)
* State game animal: [white-tailed deer](/wiki/White-tailed_deer "White-tailed deer") (since 1997)
* [State fish](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_fish "List of U.S. state fish"): [brook trout](/wiki/Brook_trout "Brook trout") (since 1965)
* [State reptile](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_reptiles "List of U.S. state reptiles"): [painted turtle](/wiki/Painted_turtle "Painted turtle") (since 1995)
* [State fossil](/wiki/State_fossil "State fossil"): [mastodon](/wiki/Mastodon "Mastodon") (since 2000)
* [State flower](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_flowers "List of U.S. state flowers"): [apple blossom](/wiki/Apple "Apple") (adopted in 1897, official in 1997)
* State wildflower: [dwarf lake iris](/wiki/Dwarf_lake_iris "Dwarf lake iris") (since 1998) a federally listed threatened species
* [State tree](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_trees "List of U.S. state trees"): [white pine](/wiki/Eastern_white_pine "Eastern white pine") (since 1955)
* [State stone](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_minerals,_rocks,_stones_and_gemstones "List of U.S. state minerals, rocks, stones and gemstones"): [Petoskey stone](/wiki/Petoskey_stone "Petoskey stone") (since 1965). It is composed of fossilized [coral](/wiki/Coral "Coral") (*Hexagonaria pericarnata*) from long ago when the middle of the continent was covered with a shallow sea.
* [State gem](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_minerals,_rocks,_stones_and_gemstones "List of U.S. state minerals, rocks, stones and gemstones"): [Isle Royale greenstone](/wiki/Chlorastrolite "Chlorastrolite") (since 1973). Also called *chlorastrolite* (literally "green star stone"), the mineral is found on [Isle Royale](/wiki/Isle_Royale "Isle Royale") and the Keweenaw peninsula.
* [State quarter](/wiki/50_State_Quarters "50 State Quarters"): US coin issued in 2004 with the Michigan motto "Great Lakes State".
* [State soil](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_soils "List of U.S. state soils"): [Kalkaska sand](/wiki/Kalkaska_sand "Kalkaska sand") (since 1990), ranges in color from black to yellowish brown, covers nearly 1,000,000-acre (4,000 km2) in 29 counties.
